title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: table summarizes recommended measures variable type. PRE = Proportional Reduction Error. measures quantify much knowing one variable reduces prediction error . functions accept contingency table (class table, typically xtabs() table()).

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: assoc_measures() computes available measures : useful exploratory analysis. reporting, pick measure matches variable types.
code: tbl <- xtabs(~ smoking + education, data = sochealth) assoc_measures(tbl) #> Measure                            Estimate     SE  CI lower  CI upper      p  #> Cramer's V                            0.136     --     0.079     0.191  <.001  #> Contingency Coefficient               0.134     --        --        --  <.001  #> Lambda symmetric                      0.000  0.000     0.000     0.000     --  #> Lambda R|C                            0.000  0.000     0.000     0.000     --  #> Lambda C|R                            0.000  0.000     0.000     0.000     --  #> Goodman-Kruskal's Tau R|C             0.018  0.008     0.003     0.034   .023  #> Goodman-Kruskal's Tau C|R             0.008  0.003     0.001     0.014   .022  #> Uncertainty Coefficient symmetric     0.011  0.005     0.002     0.021   .021  #> Uncertainty Coefficient R|C           0.018  0.008     0.003     0.032   .021  #> Uncertainty Coefficient C|R           0.009  0.004     0.001     0.016   .021  #> Goodman-Kruskal Gamma                -0.268  0.056    -0.378    -0.158  <.001  #> Kendall's Tau-b                      -0.126  0.027    -0.180    -0.073  <.001  #> Kendall's Tau-c                      -0.117  0.026    -0.167    -0.067  <.001  #> Somers' D R|C                        -0.091  0.020    -0.131    -0.052  <.001  #> Somers' D C|R                        -0.175  0.038    -0.249    -0.101  <.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Cramer’s V measures strength association two nominal variables. ranges 0 (association) 1 (perfect association). Pass detail = TRUE confidence interval p-value. p-value tests null hypothesis association using Pearson chi-squared test.
code: tbl <- xtabs(~ smoking + education, data = sochealth) cramer_v(tbl) #> [1] 0.1356677 cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.136     0.079     0.191  <.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: 2x2 tables, Phi equivalent Cramer’s V. spicy implements Phi \(\sqrt{\chi^2 / n}\), matching DescTools SPSS conventions – value always non-negative reports strength association, direction. recover signed direction, compute Pearson correlation two binary variables explicitly (e.g., cor(x, y) coding 0/1). p-value tests H0: association (Pearson chi-squared test).
code: tbl_22 <- xtabs(~ smoking + physical_activity, data = sochealth) phi(tbl_22, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper     p #>    0.006     0.000     0.063  .839

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: contingency coefficient alternative Cramer’s V. upper bound depends table dimensions, makes harder compare across tables different sizes. p-value tests H0: association (Pearson chi-squared test).
code: contingency_coef(tbl, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.134        --        --  <.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: variables ordinal (ordered factors), measures account ordering appropriate Cramer’s V.

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Gamma ranges -1 +1. ignores tied pairs, makes sensitive direction association tends overestimate strength many ties. positive value means higher values one variable tend occur higher values . p-value tests H0: Gamma = 0 using Wald z-test.
code: tbl_ord <- xtabs(~ self_rated_health + education, data = sochealth) gamma_gk(tbl_ord, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.310     0.238     0.383  <.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Tau-b adjusts ties ranges -1 +1. generally preferred Gamma square near-square tables. p-value tests H0: Tau-b = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: kendall_tau_b(tbl_ord, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.205     0.155     0.254  <.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Tau-c similar Tau-b adjusts rectangular tables number rows columns differ. p-value tests H0: Tau-c = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: kendall_tau_c(tbl_ord, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.200     0.151     0.248  <.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Somers’ D asymmetric measure: distinguishes dependent independent variable. default, row variable treated dependent (D(R|C)). p-value tests H0: D = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: somers_d(tbl_ord, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.208     0.157     0.258  <.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: measures answer specific question: much knowing column variable reduce error predicting row variable (vice versa)?

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Lambda measures proportional reduction classification error. can equal zero even variables associated, modal category change across columns. p-value tests H0: Lambda = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: tbl <- xtabs(~ self_rated_health + education, data = sochealth) lambda_gk(tbl, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper     p #>    0.012     0.000     0.039  .389

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Tau measures proportional reduction error predicting row variable column variable, using full distribution (just mode). p-value tests H0: Tau = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: goodman_kruskal_tau(tbl, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.017     0.008     0.026  <.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: uncertainty coefficient (Theil’s U) based entropy. measures much knowing one variable reduces uncertainty . p-value tests H0: U = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: uncertainty_coef(tbl, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.028     0.016     0.040  <.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: Yule’s Q defined 2x2 tables . ranges -1 +1 equivalent Gamma 2x2 tables. p-value tests H0: Q = 0 (Wald z-test).
code: tbl_22 <- xtabs(~ smoking + physical_activity, data = sochealth) yule_q(tbl_22, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper     p #>    0.015    -0.126     0.155  .839

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: cross_tab() can automatically select appropriate measure via assoc_measure = "auto" (default). variables ordered factors, picks Kendall’s Tau-b; otherwise uses Cramer’s V. can override automatic choice:

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: functions support confidence intervals via detail = TRUE. confidence level defaults 95% can changed conf_level: get estimate p-value (CI), pass conf_level = NULL:
code: cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE, conf_level = 0.99) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.176     0.103     0.248  <.001 cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE, conf_level = NULL) #> Estimate      p #>    0.176  <.001

title: Cramer's V, Phi, and association measures for contingency tables in R
text: detail = FALSE (default), functions return plain numeric scalar, R’s formatting rules apply. detail = TRUE, result uses custom print method defaults 3 decimal places. Pass digits change precision estimate, SE, CI columns. p-value follows APA-style formatting independent digits: 3 decimal places leading zero stripped (.045) <.001 threshold: digits argument works assoc_measures(): can also store result re-display different precision without recalculating:
code: cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE, digits = 4) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>   0.1762    0.1203    0.2309  <.001 assoc_measures(tbl, digits = 2) #> Measure                            Estimate    SE  CI lower  CI upper      p  #> Cramer's V                             0.18    --      0.12      0.23  <.001  #> Contingency Coefficient                0.24    --        --        --  <.001  #> Lambda symmetric                       0.01  0.01      0.00      0.04   .389  #> Lambda R|C                             0.00  0.00      0.00      0.00     --  #> Lambda C|R                             0.02  0.03      0.00      0.07   .386  #> Goodman-Kruskal's Tau R|C              0.02  0.00      0.01      0.03  <.001  #> Goodman-Kruskal's Tau C|R              0.03  0.01      0.01      0.04  <.001  #> Uncertainty Coefficient symmetric      0.03  0.01      0.02      0.04  <.001  #> Uncertainty Coefficient R|C            0.03  0.01      0.01      0.04  <.001  #> Uncertainty Coefficient C|R            0.03  0.01      0.02      0.04  <.001  #> Goodman-Kruskal Gamma                  0.31  0.04      0.24      0.38  <.001  #> Kendall's Tau-b                        0.20  0.03      0.16      0.25  <.001  #> Kendall's Tau-c                        0.20  0.02      0.15      0.25  <.001  #> Somers' D R|C                          0.21  0.03      0.16      0.26  <.001  #> Somers' D C|R                          0.20  0.02      0.15      0.25  <.001 res <- cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE) print(res, digits = 5) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>  0.17617   0.12031   0.23092  <.001

title: Frequency tables and cross-tabulations in R
text: Pass data frame variable name get counts percentages:
code: freq(sochealth, education) #> Frequency table: education #>  #>  Category   │ Values               Freq.    Percent  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ Lower secondary        261       21.8  #>             │ Upper secondary        539       44.9  #>             │ Tertiary               400       33.3  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │                       1200      100.0  #>  #> Label: Highest education level #> Class: ordered, factor #> Data: sochealth

title: Frequency tables and cross-tabulations in R
text: Sort frequency sort = "-" (decreasing) sort = "+" (increasing). Sort alphabetically sort = "name+" sort = "name-": Sort alphabetically:
code: freq(sochealth, education, sort = "-") #> Frequency table: education #>  #>  Category   │ Values               Freq.    Percent  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ Upper secondary        539       44.9  #>             │ Tertiary               400       33.3  #>             │ Lower secondary        261       21.8  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │                       1200      100.0  #>  #> Label: Highest education level #> Class: ordered, factor #> Data: sochealth freq(sochealth, education, sort = "name+") #> Frequency table: education #>  #>  Category   │ Values               Freq.    Percent  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ Lower secondary        261       21.8  #>             │ Tertiary               400       33.3  #>             │ Upper secondary        539       44.9  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │                       1200      100.0  #>  #> Label: Highest education level #> Class: ordered, factor #> Data: sochealth

title: Frequency tables and cross-tabulations in R
text: Supply weight variable weights. default, rescale = TRUE adjusts weighted total match unweighted sample size: Set rescale = FALSE keep raw weighted counts:
code: freq(sochealth, education, weights = weight) #> Frequency table: education #>  #>  Category   │ Values               Freq.    Percent  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ Lower secondary        259       21.6  #>             │ Upper secondary        546       45.5  #>             │ Tertiary               395       32.9  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │                       1200      100.0  #>  #> Label: Highest education level #> Class: ordered, factor #> Data: sochealth #> Weight: weight (rescaled) freq(sochealth, education, weights = weight, rescale = FALSE) #> Frequency table: education #>  #>  Category   │ Values               Freq.    Percent  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ Lower secondary        258       21.6  #>             │ Upper secondary        545       45.5  #>             │ Tertiary               394       32.9  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │                       1196      100.0  #>  #> Label: Highest education level #> Class: ordered, factor #> Data: sochealth #> Weight: weight

title: Frequency tables and cross-tabulations in R
text: variable value labels (e.g., imported SPSS Stata haven), freq() shows default [code] label format. Control labelled_levels:
code: # Create a labelled version of the smoking variable sh <- sochealth sh$smoking_lbl <- labelled::labelled(   ifelse(sh$smoking == "Yes", 1L, 0L),   labels = c("Non-smoker" = 0L, "Current smoker" = 1L) )  # Default: [code] label freq(sh, smoking_lbl) #> Frequency table: smoking_lbl #>  #>  Category   │ Values                  Freq.    Percent    Valid Percent  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ [0] Non-smoker            926       77.2             78.8  #>             │ [1] Current smoker        249       20.8             21.2  #>  Missing    │ NA                         25        2.1                   #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │                          1200      100.0            100.0  #>  #> Class: haven_labelled, vctrs_vctr, integer #> Data: sh  # Labels only (no codes) freq(sh, smoking_lbl, labelled_levels = "labels") #> Frequency table: smoking_lbl #>  #>  Category   │ Values              Freq.    Percent    Valid Percent  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ Non-smoker            926       77.2             78.8  #>             │ Current smoker        249       20.8             21.2  #>  Missing    │ NA                     25        2.1                   #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │                      1200      100.0            100.0  #>  #> Class: haven_labelled, vctrs_vctr, integer #> Data: sh  # Codes only (no labels) freq(sh, smoking_lbl, labelled_levels = "values") #> Frequency table: smoking_lbl #>  #>  Category   │ Values      Freq.    Percent    Valid Percent  #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Valid      │ 0             926       77.2             78.8  #>             │ 1             249       20.8             21.2  #>  Missing    │ NA             25        2.1                   #> ────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Total      │              1200      100.0            100.0  #>  #> Class: haven_labelled, vctrs_vctr, integer #> Data: sh

title: Frequency tables and cross-tabulations in R
text: Weights supplied via weights, just like freq(). default differs, however: cross_tab() defaults rescale = FALSE (raw weighted counts), whereas freq() defaults rescale = TRUE. Without rescaling, table shows raw weighted counts: Pass rescale = TRUE make weighted total match unweighted sample size:
code: cross_tab(sochealth, smoking, education, weights = weight) #> Crosstable: smoking x education (N) #>  #>  Values   │   Lower secondary    Upper secondary    Tertiary │   Total  #> ──────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────── #>  No       │               176                417         324 │     917  #>  Yes      │                79                114          60 │     253  #> ──────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────── #>  Total    │               255                531         384 │    1170  #>  #> Chi-2(2) = 21.3, p <.001 #> Cramer's V = 0.13 #> Weight: weight cross_tab(sochealth, smoking, education, weights = weight, rescale = TRUE) #> Crosstable: smoking x education (N) #>  #>  Values   │   Lower secondary    Upper secondary    Tertiary │   Total  #> ──────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────── #>  No       │               176                419         325 │     921  #>  Yes      │                79                115          60 │     254  #> ──────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────── #>  Total    │               255                534         385 │    1175  #>  #> Chi-2(2) = 21.4, p <.001 #> Cramer's V = 0.13 #> Weight: weight (rescaled)

title: Getting started with spicy
text: varlist() (shortcut vl()) gives compact overview every variable data frame: name, label, representative values, class, number distinct values, valid observations, missing values. RStudio Positron, calling varlist() without arguments opens interactive viewer - common usage practice. use tbl = TRUE produce static output vignette: can also select specific columns tidyselect syntax:
code: varlist(sochealth, tbl = TRUE) #> # A tibble: 24 × 7 #>    Variable          Label                 Values Class N_distinct N_valid   NAs #>    <chr>             <chr>                 <chr>  <chr>      <int>   <int> <int> #>  1 sex               Sex                   Femal… fact…          2    1200     0 #>  2 age               Age (years)           25, 2… nume…         51    1200     0 #>  3 age_group         Age group             25-34… orde…          4    1200     0 #>  4 education         Highest education le… Lower… orde…          3    1200     0 #>  5 social_class      Subjective social cl… Lower… orde…          5    1200     0 #>  6 region            Region of residence   Centr… fact…          6    1200     0 #>  7 employment_status Employment status     Emplo… fact…          4    1200     0 #>  8 income_group      Household income gro… Low, … orde…          4    1182    18 #>  9 income            Monthly household in… 1000,… nume…       1052    1200     0 #> 10 smoking           Current smoker        No, Y… fact…          2    1175    25 #> # ℹ 14 more rows varlist(sochealth, starts_with("bmi"), income, weight, tbl = TRUE) #> # A tibble: 4 × 7 #>   Variable     Label                       Values Class N_distinct N_valid   NAs #>   <chr>        <chr>                       <chr>  <chr>      <int>   <int> <int> #> 1 bmi          Body mass index             16, 1… nume…        177    1188    12 #> 2 bmi_category BMI category                Norma… orde…          3    1188    12 #> 3 income       Monthly household income (… 1000,… nume…       1052    1200     0 #> 4 weight       Survey design weight        0.294… nume…        794    1200     0

title: Getting started with spicy
text: quick overview available association statistics, pass contingency table assoc_measures(): Individual functions cramer_v(), gamma_gk(), kendall_tau_b() return scalar default. Pass detail = TRUE confidence interval p-value:
code: tbl <- xtabs(~ smoking + education, data = sochealth) assoc_measures(tbl) #> Measure                            Estimate     SE  CI lower  CI upper      p  #> Cramer's V                            0.136     --     0.079     0.191  <.001  #> Contingency Coefficient               0.134     --        --        --  <.001  #> Lambda symmetric                      0.000  0.000     0.000     0.000     --  #> Lambda R|C                            0.000  0.000     0.000     0.000     --  #> Lambda C|R                            0.000  0.000     0.000     0.000     --  #> Goodman-Kruskal's Tau R|C             0.018  0.008     0.003     0.034   .023  #> Goodman-Kruskal's Tau C|R             0.008  0.003     0.001     0.014   .022  #> Uncertainty Coefficient symmetric     0.011  0.005     0.002     0.021   .021  #> Uncertainty Coefficient R|C           0.018  0.008     0.003     0.032   .021  #> Uncertainty Coefficient C|R           0.009  0.004     0.001     0.016   .021  #> Goodman-Kruskal Gamma                -0.268  0.056    -0.378    -0.158  <.001  #> Kendall's Tau-b                      -0.126  0.027    -0.180    -0.073  <.001  #> Kendall's Tau-c                      -0.117  0.026    -0.167    -0.067  <.001  #> Somers' D R|C                        -0.091  0.020    -0.131    -0.052  <.001  #> Somers' D C|R                        -0.175  0.038    -0.249    -0.101  <.001 cramer_v(tbl, detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper      p #>    0.136     0.079     0.191  <.001

title: Getting started with spicy
text: table_categorical() covers grouped one-way summary tables categorical variables: table_continuous() summarizes continuous variables, either overall categorical variable, can also add group-comparison tests: table_continuous_lm() covers reporting territory want stay linear-model framework, example robust cluster-robust standard errors, case weights, additive covariate adjustment: table_regression() reports full coefficient table one several fitted lm() glm() models, APA-aligned formatting, factor grouping reference rows, robust variance, standardised coefficients, average marginal effects, hierarchical comparisons, side--side multi-model layouts: detailed guidance, see dedicated articles table_categorical(), table_continuous(), table_continuous_lm(), table_regression(), final reporting overview APA-style summary tables.

title: Getting started with spicy
text: mean_n(), sum_n(), count_n() compute row-wise statistics across selected columns, automatic handling missing values.
code: sochealth |>   dplyr::mutate(     mean_sat  = mean_n(select = starts_with("life_sat")),     sum_sat   = sum_n(select = starts_with("life_sat"), min_valid = 2),     n_missing = count_n(select = starts_with("life_sat"), special = "NA")   ) |>   dplyr::select(starts_with("life_sat"), mean_sat, sum_sat, n_missing) |>   head() |>   as.data.frame() #>   life_sat_health life_sat_work life_sat_relationships life_sat_standard #> 1               5             3                      5                 5 #> 2               4             4                      5                 5 #> 3               3             2                      5                 3 #> 4               3             4                      3                 2 #> 5               4             5                      4                 4 #> 6               5             5                      5                 3 #>   mean_sat sum_sat n_missing #> 1     4.50      18         0 #> 2     4.50      18         0 #> 3     3.25      13         0 #> 4     3.00      12         0 #> 5     4.25      17         0 #> 6     4.50      18         0

title: Getting started with spicy
text: See ?varlist inspect variables, labels, values, missing data. See ?freq one-way frequency tables (weights, sorting, custom missing values, labelled-data display modes). See ?cross_tab full list arguments (weights, simulation, association measures). See ?assoc_measures complete list association statistics; ?cramer_v canonical entry point. See ?table_categorical grouped one-way categorical tables. See ?table_continuous continuous summaries group comparisons. See ?table_continuous_lm model-based mean-comparison tables robust / cluster-robust / bootstrap / jackknife SE, case weights, additive covariate adjustment. See ?table_regression lm / glm coefficient tables APA-aligned formatting, robust variance, standardised coefficients, average marginal effects, hierarchical comparisons, side--side multi-model layouts. See ?mean_n, ?sum_n, ?count_n row-wise summaries optional minimum-valid-values rules. See ?code_book generate interactive HTML codebook; ?label_from_names derive variable labels "code. label"-style column names (e.g., LimeSurvey exports).

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: Use function matches unit want report: practice, follow APA sequence: start table_categorical() smoking, education, activity — APA Table 1 categorical descriptors; use table_continuous() BMI, well-, income — Table 1 continuous descriptors Table 2 unadjusted group comparisons; switch table_continuous_lm() comparison must account survey weights, robust SE, covariate adjustment; finish table_regression() substantive model fitted — APA Table 3 predictors, factor groupings, reference rows, (optionally) standardised coefficients, marginal effects, nested model comparisons. first three functions live inside select / data-frame grammar; table_regression() fit-first — build model usual R way (lm() glm()) hand object . four share post-construction grammar (output, labels, digits, decimal_mark, align), swapping functions never breaks rendering pipeline.

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: three descriptive functions share core arguments: argument pattern used three cases: select chooses reported variables; defines grouping structure; labels cleans row labels; output decides result rendered exported. model-based continuous tables, pattern applies, must single predictor one linear model fit per outcome. table_regression() joins labels / output / decimal_mark / digits grammar fit-first: rather expressing model structure inline select , pass one several already-fitted lm() glm() objects: Linear regression: wellbeing_score split intentional. descriptive trio (categorical, continuous, continuous_lm) reports data — select describe want see. table_regression() reports model — model formula already declared predictors, interactions, polynomials, transformations, splines, contrasts report, passing select / duplicate model object’s information risk diverging .
code: table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   by = education,   labels = c("Smoking status", "Regular physical activity"),   output = "tinytable" ) table_continuous(   sochealth,   select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score, life_sat_health),   by = education,   labels = c(     bmi = "Body mass index",     wellbeing_score = "Well-being score",     life_sat_health = "Satisfaction with health"   ),   output = "tinytable" ) table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score, life_sat_health),   by = education,   weights = weight ) #> Continuous outcomes by Highest education level #>  #>  Variable                       │ M (Lower secondary)  M (Upper secondary)  #> ────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Body mass index                │        25.96                23.39         #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100)  │        67.55                80.88         #>  Satisfaction with health (1-5) │         3.45                 4.39         #>  #>  Variable                       │ M (Tertiary)    p     R²    n    #> ────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────── #>  Body mass index                │    26.16      <.001  0.13  1188  #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100)  │    66.52      <.001  0.19  1200  #>  Satisfaction with health (1-5) │     3.39      <.001  0.15  1192 fit <- lm(   wellbeing_score ~ age + sex + smoking + physical_activity,   data = sochealth ) table_regression(   fit,   labels = c(     age               = "Age (years)",     sex               = "Sex",     smoking           = "Smoking status",     physical_activity = "Regular physical activity"   ),   output = "tinytable" )

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: common report contains table types, often grouping variable. example, might first summarize categorical health behaviors, summarize continuous well-indicators.

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = c(smoking, physical_activity, dentist_12m),     by = education,     labels = c(       "Smoking status",       "Regular physical activity",       "Visited a dentist in the last 12 months"     ),     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: keeps reporting structure consistent still using function fits variable type.
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_continuous(     sochealth,     select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score, life_sat_health),     by = education,     labels = c(       bmi = "Body mass index",       wellbeing_score = "Well-being score",       life_sat_health = "Satisfaction with health"     ),     p_value = TRUE,     effect_size = TRUE,     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: better summary-table path article already organized around simple linear models, weighted analyses, robust standard errors.
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_continuous_lm(     sochealth,     select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score, life_sat_health),     by = sex,     vcov = "HC3",     statistic = TRUE,     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text:
substantive model fitted, table_regression() produces APA Table 3 coefficient summary. output argument controls rendering, regression table sits reporting pipeline descriptive ones : #uelzcunknk table { font-family: system-ui, ‘Segoe UI’, Roboto, Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif, ‘Apple Color Emoji’, ‘Segoe UI Emoji’, ‘Segoe UI Symbol’, ‘Noto Color Emoji’; -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased; -moz-osx-font-smoothing: grayscale; }

.gt_table, .gt_heading, .gt_col_headings, .gt_col_heading, .gt_column_spanner_outer, .gt_column_spanner, .gt_title, .gt_subtitle, .gt_sourcenotes, .gt_sourcenote { background-color: transparent !important; color: currentColor !important; } Average marginal effects (ame) useful next odds ratio report probability-scale change predictor — quantity reviewers want interpret directly.
code: fit <- lm(   wellbeing_score ~ age + sex + smoking + physical_activity,   data = sochealth ) pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_regression(     fit,     standardized = "refit",     show_columns = c("b", "beta", "ci", "p"),     vcov = "HC3",     output = "gt"   ) ) fit_unadj <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ smoking, data = sochealth) fit_adj   <- lm(   wellbeing_score ~ smoking + age + sex + physical_activity,   data = sochealth ) pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_regression(     list("Unadjusted" = fit_unadj, "Adjusted" = fit_adj),     show_columns = c("b", "ci", "p"),     output = "gt"   ) ) fit_glm <- glm(   smoking ~ age + sex + physical_activity,   data = sochealth,   family = binomial() ) pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_regression(     fit_glm,     exponentiate = TRUE,     show_columns = c("b", "ci", "p", "ame", "ame_ci"),     output = "gt"   ) ) #> Warning: `"ame"` and `"p"` shown without `"ame_p"`: the `p` column is for B (or beta), not the AME. They can differ under non-linear links or interactions. #> ℹ Add `"ame_p"` to display the AME-specific p-value.

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: four functions support reporting formats: Pick output based table going, analysis . underlying selection grouping pattern stays . want object fits naturally Word PowerPoint workflows can also rendered HTML documents, flextable good choice:
code: if (requireNamespace("flextable", quietly = TRUE)) {   table_continuous(     sochealth,     select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score, life_sat_health),     by = education,     output = "flextable"   ) }

title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: four summary-table helpers return regular gt, tinytable, flextable objects, can keep styling native package API. includes table_regression(): nothing fit-first interface changes rendering engine produces. Use gt:: functions want keep gt workflow: Use tinytable:: functions want lightweight table-specific styling: Use flextable:: functions want keep working toward Office HTML document output. example shown code dark pkgdown theme reliable preview final flextable HTML rendering:
code: tab <- pkgdown_dark_gt(table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   by = education,   labels = c("Smoking status", "Regular physical activity"),   output = "gt" ))  tab |>   gt::tab_header(     title = "Health behaviors by education",     subtitle = "Categorical summary table"   ) |>   gt::tab_source_note(     gt::md("*Percentages are computed within each education group.*")   ) tab <- table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   by = education,   labels = c("Smoking status", "Regular physical activity"),   output = "tinytable" )  tab |>   tinytable::style_tt(     i = 2:3,     j = 2:5,     background = "red",     color = "white",     bold = TRUE   ) if (requireNamespace("flextable", quietly = TRUE)) {   tab <- table_continuous(     sochealth,     select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score),     by = education,     output = "flextable"   )    tab |>     flextable::theme_booktabs() |>     flextable::autofit() |>     flextable::fontsize(size = 10, part = "all") }

what: Keep the detailed options in the function-specific articles
title: Summary tables for APA-style reporting
text: dedicated articles go deeper function: table_categorical() covers missing values, level filtering, association measures, one-way frequency-style tables. table_continuous() covers grouped descriptive statistics, parametric nonparametric tests, effect sizes. table_continuous_lm() covers estimated marginal means slopes linear models, robust / cluster-robust / bootstrap / jackknife variance, case weights, additive covariate adjustment (G-computation equal-weight), four effect-size families noncentral CIs. table_regression() covers single- multi-model coefficient tables lm / glm, four standardisation methods, partial effect sizes noncentral-F CIs, average marginal effects, hierarchical (nested = TRUE) comparisons, multiplicity correction, response-scale reporting GLMs. Use vignette final reporting overview, consult function-specific articles need detailed controls.

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: grouped tables, provide data frame, one selected variables, grouping variable: default output "default", prints styled ASCII table console. Use output = "data.frame" get plain numeric data frame suitable processing.

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: Omit build frequency-style table selected variables:
code: table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   output = "default" ) #> Categorical table #>  #>  Variable            │   n      %     #> ─────────────────────┼─────────────── #>  smoking             │                #>    No                │  926    78.8   #>    Yes               │  249    21.2   #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  physical_activity   │                #>    No                │  650    54.2   #>    Yes               │  550    45.8

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: table_categorical() supports several output formats. table summarizes options:

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: "gt" format produces table APA-style borders, column spanners, proper alignment:
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = c(smoking, physical_activity, dentist_12m),     by = education,     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Categorical summary tables in R
code: table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   by = sex,   output = "tinytable" )

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: Use output = "data.frame" wide numeric data frame (one row per modality), output = "long" long format (one row per modality x group):
code: table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = smoking,   by = education,   output = "data.frame" ) #>   Variable Level Lower secondary n Lower secondary % Upper secondary n #> 1  smoking    No               179              69.6               415 #> 2  smoking   Yes                78              30.4               112 #>   Upper secondary % Tertiary n Tertiary % Total n Total %            p #> 1              78.7        332       84.9     926    78.8 2.012877e-05 #> 2              21.3         59       15.1     249    21.2 2.012877e-05 #>   Cramer's V #> 1  0.1356677 #> 2  0.1356677

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: default, table_categorical() uses variable names row headers. Use labels argument provide human-readable labels. Two forms accepted (matching table_continuous() table_continuous_lm()): named character vector keyed column name data – recommended form. listed columns relabelled; others fall back column name. positional character vector length select – legacy spicy < 0.11.0 form, kept backward compatibility.
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = c(smoking, physical_activity),     by = education,     labels = c(       smoking           = "Smoking status",       physical_activity = "Regular physical activity"     ),     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: table_categorical() picks association measure per row variable based variable type (assoc_measure = "auto", default): 2x2 (binary row variable vs. binary ) -> phi, ordered factors -> Kendall’s tau_b, otherwise -> Cramer’s V. chosen measures differ across rows, column header collapses "Effect size" APA-style Note. line documents measure used variable. Override single string uniform application, named vector mix measures per row: Add confidence intervals assoc_ci = TRUE. rendered formats (gt, tinytable, flextable, word), CI shown inline: data formats ("data.frame", "long", "excel", "clipboard"), separate CI lower CI upper columns added:
code: # Uniform: same measure for every row variable table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = smoking,   by = education,   assoc_measure = "lambda",   output = "tinytable" ) # Per-row: pick the right measure for each variable. # `smoking` x `education` is 2x3 (binary x ordered) -> Cramer's V; # `self_rated_health` x `education` is ordered x ordered -> Tau-b. # The mixed result collapses the header to "Effect size" and adds an # APA `Note.` line documenting the per-row measure. table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, self_rated_health),   by = education,   assoc_measure = c(     smoking           = "cramer_v",     self_rated_health = "tau_b"   ),   output = "tinytable" ) pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = c(smoking, physical_activity),     by = education,     assoc_ci = TRUE,     output = "gt"   ) ) table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = smoking,   by = education,   assoc_ci = TRUE,   output = "data.frame" ) #>   Variable Level Lower secondary n Lower secondary % Upper secondary n #> 1  smoking    No               179              69.6               415 #> 2  smoking   Yes                78              30.4               112 #>   Upper secondary % Tertiary n Tertiary % Total n Total %            p #> 1              78.7        332       84.9     926    78.8 2.012877e-05 #> 2              21.3         59       15.1     249    21.2 2.012877e-05 #>   Cramer's V   CI lower  CI upper #> 1  0.1356677 0.07909264 0.1913716 #> 2  0.1356677 0.07909264 0.1913716

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: Pass survey weights weights argument. Use rescale = TRUE total weighted N matches unweighted N:
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = c(smoking, physical_activity),     by = education,     weights = "weight",     rescale = TRUE,     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: default, rows missing values dropped (drop_na = TRUE). Set drop_na = FALSE display “(Missing)” category:
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = income_group,     by = education,     drop_na = FALSE,     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: Use levels_keep display specific modalities. order specify controls display order, useful placing “(Missing)” first highlight missingness:
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = income_group,     by = education,     drop_na = FALSE,     levels_keep = c("(Missing)", "Low", "High"),     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: Control number digits percentages, p-values, association measure: p_digits drives displayed precision p column small-p threshold (p_digits = 3 -> <.001, p_digits = 4 -> <.0001), matching table_continuous() table_continuous_lm().
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_categorical(     sochealth,     select = smoking,     by = education,     percent_digits = 2,     p_digits = 4,     v_digits = 3,     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: default (align = "decimal") numeric columns aligned decimal mark, standard scientific-publication convention used SPSS, SAS, LaTeX siunitx, native primitives gt::cols_align_decimal() tinytable::style_tt(align = "d"). Engines without native primitive (flextable, word, clipboard, ASCII print) get alignment via leading / trailing space padding, centring default body font — single-font policy table_regression(). "center" "right" apply literal alignment:

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: table_categorical() returns object can coerced plain data.frame / tbl_df (stripping spicy formatting attributes) piped broom::tidy() / broom::glance() downstream tidyverse-stats workflow:
code: out <- table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   by = sex ) #> Categorical table by sex #>  #>  Variable          │ Female n  Female %  Male n  Male %  Total n  Total %   p    #> ───────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  smoking           │                                                       .713  #>    No              │   475       78.4     451     79.3     926     78.8          #>    Yes             │   131       21.6     118     20.7     249     21.2          #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  physical_activity │                                                       .832  #>    No              │   334       53.9     316     54.5     650     54.2          #>    Yes             │   286       46.1     264     45.5     550     45.8          #>  #>  Variable          │ Phi  #> ───────────────────┼───── #>  smoking           │ .01  #>    No              │      #>    Yes             │      #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌ #>  physical_activity │ .01  #>    No              │      #>    Yes             │  # One row per (variable x level x group) with broom-style columns # (outcome, level, group, n, proportion). The synthetic Total # margin is excluded so each observation is counted once. broom::tidy(out) #> # A tibble: 8 × 5 #>   outcome           level group      n proportion #>   <chr>             <chr> <chr>  <int>      <dbl> #> 1 smoking           No    Female   475      0.784 #> 2 smoking           No    Male     451      0.793 #> 3 smoking           Yes   Female   131      0.216 #> 4 smoking           Yes   Male     118      0.207 #> 5 physical_activity No    Female   334      0.539 #> 6 physical_activity No    Male     316      0.545 #> 7 physical_activity Yes   Female   286      0.461 #> 8 physical_activity Yes   Male     264      0.455  # One row per outcome with the omnibus chi-squared test and the # chosen association measure (test_type, statistic, df, p.value, # assoc_type, assoc_value, assoc_ci_lower / assoc_ci_upper, n_total). broom::glance(out) #> # A tibble: 2 × 10 #>   outcome           test_type   statistic    df p.value assoc_type assoc_value #>   <chr>             <chr>           <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>            <dbl> #> 1 physical_activity chi_squared    0.0452     1   0.832 Phi            0.00614 #> 2 smoking           chi_squared    0.136      1   0.713 Phi            0.0107  #> # ℹ 3 more variables: assoc_ci_lower <dbl>, assoc_ci_upper <dbl>, n_total <int>

what: Exporting to Excel, Word, or clipboard
title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: Excel export, provide file path: Word, use output = "word": can also copy directly clipboard pasting spreadsheet text editor:
code: table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity, dentist_12m),   by = education,   output = "excel",   excel_path = "my_table.xlsx" ) table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity, dentist_12m),   by = education,   output = "word",   word_path = "my_table.docx" ) table_categorical(   sochealth,   select = c(smoking, physical_activity),   by = education,   output = "clipboard" )

title: Categorical summary tables in R
text: See vignette("table-continuous", package = "spicy") continuous counterpart classical group-comparison tests. See vignette("table-continuous-lm", package = "spicy") model-based continuous summary tables. See vignette("table-regression", package = "spicy") full coefficient table one several fitted lm / glm models (APA Table 3). See vignette("summary-tables-reporting", package = "spicy") cross-function reporting workflow ties four summary-table helpers together along APA Table 1 / 2 / 3 sequence.

title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: Use select one continuous outcomes single predictor: categorical predictors, table reports estimated means level. predictor dichotomous, can also show single mean difference confidence interval.
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi, life_sat_health),   by = sex ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                       │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100)  │   67.16      71.05          3.89         #>  Body mass index                │   25.69      26.20          0.51         #>  Satisfaction with health (1-5) │    3.51       3.59          0.08         #>  #>  Variable                       │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²    n    #> ────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100)  │    2.13      5.64     <.001  0.02  1200  #>  Body mass index                │    0.09      0.93      .018  0.00  1188  #>  Satisfaction with health (1-5) │   -0.06      0.22      .267  0.00  1192

title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: Use vcov = "HC*" want heteroskedasticity-consistent standard errors tests: HC* family computed via [sandwich::vcovHC()] includes "HC0", "HC1", "HC2", "HC3" (default small / moderate samples), "HC4", "HC4m", "HC5".
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   vcov = "HC3",   statistic = TRUE ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   67.16      71.05          3.89         #>  Body mass index               │   25.69      26.20          0.51         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL   t      p     R²    n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2.12       5.65     4.32  <.001  0.02  1200  #>  Body mass index               │   0.09       0.93     2.38   .018  0.00  1188

title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: observations independent (repeated measurements per individual, students nested classes, panel data), classical HC* standard errors biased downward. Use CR* family together cluster = id_var get cluster-robust inference, dispatched clubSandwich: "CR2" recommended default (Bell & McCaffrey 2002; Pustejovsky & Tipton 2018). produces fractional Satterthwaite degrees freedom, rendered displayed test header e.g. t(8.7) F(2, 12.4). "CR1" matches Stata’s vce(cluster id) default.
code: table_continuous_lm(   sleep,   select = extra,   by = group,   vcov = "CR2",   cluster = ID,   statistic = TRUE ) #> Continuous outcomes by group #>  #>  Variable │ M (1)  M (2)  Δ (2 - 1)  95% CI LL  95% CI UL  t(9)   p     R²   n   #> ──────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  extra    │ 0.75   2.33     1.58       0.70       2.46     4.06  .003  0.16  20

title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: situations residual distribution non-standard sample small, vcov = "bootstrap" vcov = "jackknife" provide resampling-based variance estimators pure base R (dependency added): cluster supplied, bootstrap switches cluster bootstrap (Cameron, Gelbach & Miller 2008) jackknife leave-one-cluster-(Quenouille 1956). estimators use asymptotic inference: z single-coefficient contrasts chi^2(q) global Wald test k > 2 categorical predictors, rendered displayed test header.
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = wellbeing_score,   by = sex,   vcov = "bootstrap",   boot_n = 1000  # default )

title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: Use weights want weighted estimated means slopes model-based table: often natural summary-table function reporting workflow already relies weighted linear models.
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = education,   weights = weight,   show_weighted_n = TRUE ) #> Continuous outcomes by Highest education level #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Lower secondary)  M (Upper secondary)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │        67.55                80.88         #>  Body mass index               │        25.96                23.39         #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Tertiary)    p     R²    n    Weighted n  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │    66.52      <.001  0.19  1200   1196.47    #>  Body mass index               │    26.16      <.001  0.13  1188   1183.32

title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: numeric, table_continuous_lm() reports slope rather group means: need underlying returned data processing, use output = "data.frame" wide raw table output = "long" analytic long table.
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = age,   vcov = "HC3" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Age (years) #>  #>  Variable                      │  B    95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²    n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 0.04    -0.02      0.10      .176  0.00  1200  #>  Body mass index               │ 0.04     0.02      0.05     <.001  0.02  1188

title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: comparison -group means potentially confounded variables, supply covariates. per-outcome linear model becomes lm(outcome ~ + covariate1 + covariate2 + ...), reported Δ (categorical ) slope (numeric ), CI, p, R² effect size adjusted covariates. default ASCII print emits APA-style footer naming adjustment reader knows estimand reading. covariates accepts full tidyselect vocabulary (c(age, sex), tidyselect::starts_with("life_sat_"), tidyselect::all_of(cov_names)) literal character vector. covariate also appears select silently auto-excluded outcome list (mirroring / weights excluded select); covariate equal rejected front.
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   covariates = c(age, education),   vcov = "HC3" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   67.27      70.93          3.65         #>  Body mass index               │   25.65      26.23          0.58         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²    n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2.08       5.23     <.001  0.22  1200  #>  Body mass index               │   0.19       0.97      .004  0.16  1188  #>  #> Note. Adjusted for age, education (proportional).

what: Two estimands for the adjusted marginal means
title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: Two methodologically distinct conventions exist computing covariate-adjusted per-group means displayed table (M (level) columns printed output, emmean / emmean_se / emmean_ci_lower / emmean_ci_upper columns output = "long" data frame) categorical. table_continuous_lm() exposes choice via adjustment; valid produce different numerical answers least one factor covariate non-uniform observed proportions. adjustment = "proportional" (default) — G-computation / population-weighted standardisation. focal level , predictions evaluated every observation data replaced level (covariates kept observed values), averaged. convention Stata’s margins marginaleffects::avg_predictions(variables). Interpretation: “predicted mean population everyone = lvl, holding actual covariate profile?”. Best causal / counterfactual reporting. adjustment = "balanced" — equal-weight averaging synthetic grid. Factor-covariate level combinations crossed weighted uniformly (1 / k); numeric covariates fixed sample mean. convention emmeans::emmeans() (default), SPSS UNIANOVA EMMEANS, SAS LSMEANS. Interpretation: “predicted mean assuming balanced covariate design?”. Best descriptive / ANOVA-style APA reporting. two methods coincide trivially covariates NULL, also covariates numeric logical (factor levels expand ). diverge categorical covariate proportions skewed observed data.
code: # Same model, balanced (emmeans-style) marginal means: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   covariates = c(age, education),   adjustment = "balanced",   vcov = "HC3" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   69.93      73.59          3.65         #>  Body mass index               │   25.04      25.62          0.58         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²    n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2.08       5.23     <.001  0.22  1200  #>  Body mass index               │   0.19       0.97      .004  0.16  1188  #>  #> Note. Adjusted for age, education (balanced).

title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: Adding covariates changes effect sizes remain meaningful: effect_size = "f2" "omega2" reported partial f² / partial ω² via stats::drop1() restricted focal term — correct generalisation bivariate effect size covariate-adjusted model. effect_size = "d" "g" raise spicy_unsupported: Cohen’s d Hedges’ g defined extension adjustment (pooled SD canonical analogue). error message points f2 / omega2 partial-F generalisations. effect-size variants noncentral CIs (effect_size_ci = TRUE) computed fitted model displayed coefficients remain invariant vcov (HC* / CR* / bootstrap / jackknife affect contrast SE).

title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: covariates accepts additive terms . Formula syntax interactions transforms (covariates = ~ age * sex, covariates = ~ (age^2)) raises spicy_unsupported migration hint. Interactions polynomials reserved future spicy release. fit fully-specified model right now, lm() directly inspect via summary() table_regression() (accepts interaction / polynomial / () terms natively).

title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: table_continuous_lm() supports four effect sizes via effect_size argument. derived fitted model displayed coefficients R², table stays internally consistent — invariant vcov (choice classical HC* standard errors changes contrast SE, CI, test statistic standardized effect size ). Cohen’s d Hedges’ g conventions two-group comparisons (Cohen 1988; Hedges Olkin 1985; APA 2020) require exactly two non-empty levels: Hedges’ g applies small-sample correction J = 1 - 3/(4·df_resid - 1). generally preferred raw d published reports (Goulet-Pelletier Cousineau 2018): categorical predictors three levels (numeric predictors), use Hays’ omega² less biased estimate population variance explained (Hays 1963; Olejnik Algina 2003; Lakens 2013): Cohen’s f² (= R² / (1 - R²)) effect size familiar power-analysis frameworks (e.g. G*Power) defined predictor type:
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = smoking,   effect_size = "d" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Current smoker #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (No)  M (Yes)  Δ (Yes - No)  95% CI LL  #> ───────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 69.36    67.65      -1.72        -3.91    #>  Body mass index               │ 25.96    25.93      -0.03        -0.55    #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI UL   p     R²     d     n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   0.47     .124  0.00  -0.11  1175  #>  Body mass index               │   0.49     .905  0.00  -0.01  1163 table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = smoking,   effect_size = "g" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Current smoker #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (No)  M (Yes)  Δ (Yes - No)  95% CI LL  #> ───────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 69.36    67.65      -1.72        -3.91    #>  Body mass index               │ 25.96    25.93      -0.03        -0.55    #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI UL   p     R²     g     n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   0.47     .124  0.00  -0.11  1175  #>  Body mass index               │   0.49     .905  0.00  -0.01  1163 table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = education,   effect_size = "omega2" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Highest education level #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Lower secondary)  M (Upper secondary)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │        67.68                81.56         #>  Body mass index               │        26.17                23.55         #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Tertiary)    p     R²    ω²    n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │    66.10      <.001  0.21  0.21  1200  #>  Body mass index               │    26.35      <.001  0.13  0.13  1188 table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = age,   effect_size = "f2" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Age (years) #>  #>  Variable                      │  B    95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²    f²   #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 0.04    -0.02      0.10      .177  0.00  0.00  #>  Body mass index               │ 0.04     0.02      0.05     <.001  0.02  0.02  #>  #>  Variable                      │  n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 1200  #>  Body mass index               │ 1188

title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: Setting effect_size_ci = TRUE adds confidence interval effect size. implementation uses modern noncentral-distribution inversion approach — consensus standard commercial statistical software (Stata esize / estat esize, SAS PROC TTEST PROC GLM EFFECTSIZE) mainstream R packages (effectsize, MOTE, TOSTER, effsize): Noncentral t inversion "d" "g" (Steiger Fouladi 1997; Goulet-Pelletier Cousineau 2018; Cousineau Goulet-Pelletier 2021), nominal coverage across sample sizes — unlike older Hedges-Olkin normal approximation biased small samples. Noncentral F inversion "omega2" "f2" (Steiger 2004; Smithson 2003). printed rendered outputs, effect size displayed bracketed CI (article-ready): CI level follows ci_level (default 0.95). programmatic access, wide raw output exposes separate numeric columns, long output always includes : weights supplied, four effect sizes (CIs) computed weighted least-squares fit, keeping consistent weighted contrast CI (DuMouchel Duncan 1983). right convention case-weighted reporting; propensity-score balance assessment (Austin Stuart 2015) complex-survey designs, dedicated packages (cobalt::bal.tab() survey) appropriate.
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = smoking,   effect_size = "g",   effect_size_ci = TRUE ) #> Continuous outcomes by Current smoker #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (No)  M (Yes)  Δ (Yes - No)  95% CI LL  #> ───────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 69.36    67.65      -1.72        -3.91    #>  Body mass index               │ 25.96    25.93      -0.03        -0.55    #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI UL   p     R²            g           #> ───────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   0.47     .124  0.00  -0.11 [-0.25, 0.03]  #>  Body mass index               │   0.49     .905  0.00  -0.01 [-0.15, 0.13]  #>  #>  Variable                      │  n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 1175  #>  Body mass index               │ 1163 table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = wellbeing_score,   by = smoking,   effect_size = "g",   effect_size_ci = TRUE,   output = "data.frame" ) #>                                      Variable   M (No)  M (Yes) Δ (Yes - No) #> wellbeing_score WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) 69.36317 67.64538    -1.717793 #>                 95% CI LL 95% CI UL         p          R²          g #> wellbeing_score -3.906437 0.4708504 0.1238546 0.002017481 -0.1098571 #>                 effect_size_ci_lower effect_size_ci_upper    n #> wellbeing_score           -0.2497291           0.03006164 1175 out <- table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = wellbeing_score,   by = smoking,   effect_size = "g",   effect_size_ci = TRUE,   output = "long" ) out[, c("variable", "es_type", "es_value", "es_ci_lower", "es_ci_upper")] #>          variable es_type   es_value es_ci_lower es_ci_upper #> 1 wellbeing_score       g -0.1098571  -0.2497291  0.03006164 #> 2 wellbeing_score    <NA>         NA          NA          NA

title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: Pretty outcome labels comma decimal separator (useful European reporting):
code: table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   labels = c(     wellbeing_score = "WHO-5 wellbeing (0-100)",     bmi = "Body-mass index (kg/m²)"   ),   effect_size = "g",   effect_size_ci = TRUE,   decimal_mark = "," ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  95% CI LL  #> ─────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing (0-100) │   67,16      71,05          3,89           2,13     #>  Body-mass index (kg/m²) │   25,69      26,20          0,51           0,09     #>  #>  Variable                │ 95% CI UL    p     R²           g           n    #> ─────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing (0-100) │   5,64     <,001  0,02  0,25 [0,14; 0,36]  1200  #>  Body-mass index (kg/m²) │   0,93      ,018  0,00  0,14 [0,02; 0,25]  1188

title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: function supports output formats summary-table helpers, including tinytable, gt, flextable, excel, word, clipboard.
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_continuous_lm(     sochealth,     select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi, life_sat_health),     by = sex,     vcov = "HC3",     statistic = TRUE,     output = "gt"   ) )

title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: table_continuous_lm() returns object can coerced plain data.frame / tbl_df (stripping spicy formatting attributes) piped broom::tidy() / broom::glance() downstream tidyverse-stats workflow:
code: out <- table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   effect_size = "g",   effect_size_ci = TRUE ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   67.16      71.05          3.89         #>  Body mass index               │   25.69      26.20          0.51         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²   #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2.13       5.64     <.001  0.02  #>  Body mass index               │   0.09       0.93      .018  0.00  #>  #>  Variable                      │         g           n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 0.25 [0.14, 0.36]  1200  #>  Body mass index               │ 0.14 [0.02, 0.25]  1188  # One row per estimated parameter: emmean per level, contrast for # binary predictors, slope for numeric predictors. broom::tidy(out) #> # A tibble: 6 × 10 #>   outcome        label term  estimate_type estimate std.error conf.low conf.high #>   <chr>          <chr> <chr> <chr>            <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 wellbeing_sco… WHO-… Fema… emmean          67.2       0.623  65.9       68.4   #> 2 wellbeing_sco… WHO-… Male  emmean          71.0       0.644  69.8       72.3   #> 3 wellbeing_sco… WHO-… Male… difference       3.89      0.896   2.13       5.64  #> 4 bmi            Body… Fema… emmean          25.7       0.150  25.4       26.0   #> 5 bmi            Body… Male  emmean          26.2       0.155  25.9       26.5   #> 6 bmi            Body… Male… difference       0.512     0.216   0.0888     0.935 #> # ℹ 2 more variables: statistic <dbl>, p.value <dbl>  # One row per outcome with model-level statistics: r.squared, # adj.r.squared, F / t, df, p.value, nobs, weighted_n, plus the # effect-size summary. broom::glance(out) #> # A tibble: 2 × 16 #>   outcome     label predictor_type test_type statistic    df df.residual p.value #>   <chr>       <chr> <chr>          <chr>         <dbl> <int>       <dbl>   <dbl> #> 1 wellbeing_… WHO-… categorical    F             18.8      1        1198 1.55e-5 #> 2 bmi         Body… categorical    F              5.64     1        1186 1.78e-2 #> # ℹ 8 more variables: r.squared <dbl>, adj.r.squared <dbl>, es_type <chr>, #> #   es_value <dbl>, es_ci_lower <dbl>, es_ci_upper <dbl>, nobs <int>, #> #   weighted_n <dbl>

title: Model-based continuous summary tables in R
text: See vignette("table-continuous", package = "spicy") descriptive continuous summary tables classical group-comparison tests. See vignette("table-regression", package = "spicy") full coefficient table analysis moves per-outcome one-predictor model single multi-predictor lm / glm, including interactions, polynomials, standardised coefficients. See vignette("summary-tables-reporting", package = "spicy") cross-function reporting workflow using summary-table helpers along APA Table 1 / 2 / 3 sequence.

title: Continuous summary tables in R
text: Add summarize variables across categories. supplied, Welch-test p-value column added automatically: main pattern reporting continuous variables across groups education, sex, treatment arm, survey wave. Pass p_value = FALSE suppress test column keep output strictly descriptive. want outcomes reported linear-model workflow, heteroskedasticity-consistent cluster-robust standard errors, case weights, additive covariate adjustment, use table_continuous_lm() instead:

what: Add test statistics and effect sizes
title: Continuous summary tables in R
text: Grouped tables can also report test statistic effect size alongside default p-value column: Use test = "student" equal-variance parametric tests test = "nonparametric" rank-based comparisons: effect_size = TRUE auto-selects canonical measure chosen test number groups: Hedges’ g (parametric, 2 groups), eta-squared (parametric, 3+ groups), rank-biserial r (nonparametric, 2 groups), epsilon-squared (nonparametric, 3+ groups). pick specific measure explicitly, pass character name: Allowed values "none" (default), "auto" (= legacy TRUE), "hedges_g", "eta_sq", "r_rb", "epsilon_sq". Incompatible explicit choices (e.g. "eta_sq" two groups, "hedges_g" test = "nonparametric") trigger actionable error. need underlying columns processing, use output = "data.frame":

title: Continuous summary tables in R
text: select supports tidyselect helpers: programmatic selection, set regex = TRUE: Use exclude want broad selection one two explicit removals:

title: Continuous summary tables in R
text: Use labels replace technical variable names reporting labels: table_continuous() supports reporting-oriented outputs table_categorical(): example, tinytable works well Quarto R Markdown documents:
code: pkgdown_dark_gt(   table_continuous(     sochealth,     select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score, life_sat_health),     by = education,     labels = c(       bmi = "Body mass index",       wellbeing_score = "Well-being score",       life_sat_health = "Satisfaction with health"     ),     output = "gt"   ) ) table_continuous(   sochealth,   select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score, life_sat_health),   by = education,   output = "tinytable" )

title: Continuous summary tables in R
text: Use function export table directly:
code: table_continuous(   sochealth,   select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score, life_sat_health),   by = education,   output = "excel",   excel_path = "table_continuous.xlsx" )  table_continuous(   sochealth,   select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score, life_sat_health),   by = education,   output = "word",   word_path = "table_continuous.docx" )

title: Continuous summary tables in R
text: printed ASCII table every rendered output share formatting vocabulary table_continuous_lm() table_categorical(): align = "decimal" (default) aligns numeric columns decimal mark, matching SPSS / SAS / LaTeX siunitx conventions. "center", "right", legacy "auto" alternatives. p_digits = 3 (default, APA standard) drives displayed precision p column small-p threshold (p_digits = 4 -> .0451 <.0001). show_n = FALSE ci = FALSE drop corresponding columns (CI spanner / borders) structurally every output; underlying n ci_lower / ci_upper always present output = "data.frame" / "long" downstream code.
code: table_continuous(   sochealth,   select = wellbeing_score,   by = sex,   ci = FALSE,   show_n = FALSE,   p_digits = 4 ) #> Descriptive statistics #>  #>  Variable                      │ Group     M     SD     Min    Max      p     #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ Female  67.16  14.80  19.60  100.00  <.0001  #>                                │ Male    71.05  16.23  18.70  100.00

code: out <- table_continuous(   sochealth,   select = c(bmi, wellbeing_score),   by = sex ) #> Descriptive statistics #>  #>  Variable                      │ Group     M     SD     Min    Max    95% CI LL  #> ───────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  Body mass index               │ Female  25.69   3.78  16.00   38.90    25.39    #>                                │ Male    26.20   3.64  16.00   37.70    25.90    #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ Female  67.16  14.80  19.60  100.00    65.99    #>                                │ Male    71.05  16.23  18.70  100.00    69.73    #>  #>  Variable                      │ Group   95% CI UL   n     p    #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────── #>  Body mass index               │ Female    25.98    616   .018  #>                                │ Male      26.50    572         #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ Female    68.33    620  <.001  #>                                │ Male      72.37    580  # Long descriptive rows: one per (variable x group) with broom-style # columns (outcome, label, group, estimate = mean, std.error, # conf.low / conf.high, n, min, max, sd). broom::tidy(out) #> # A tibble: 4 × 11 #>   outcome    label group estimate std.error conf.low conf.high     n   min   max #>   <chr>      <chr> <chr>    <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> <int> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 bmi        Body… Fema…     25.7     0.152     25.4      26.0   616  16    38.9 #> 2 bmi        Body… Male      26.2     0.152     25.9      26.5   572  16    37.7 #> 3 wellbeing… WHO-… Fema…     67.2     0.594     66.0      68.3   620  19.6 100   #> 4 wellbeing… WHO-… Male      71.0     0.674     69.7      72.4   580  18.7 100   #> #
