title: Continuous-outcome linear-model table — table_continuous_lm
code: # --- Basic usage ---------------------------------------------------------  # Default: ASCII table with model-based means, p, and \eqn{R^2}{R^2}. table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   67.16      71.05          3.89         #>  Body mass index               │   25.69      26.20          0.51         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²    n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2.13       5.64     <.001  0.02  1200  #>  Body mass index               │   0.09       0.93      .018  0.00  1188   # --- Effect sizes -------------------------------------------------------  # Cohen's d (binary by required). table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   effect_size = "d" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   67.16      71.05          3.89         #>  Body mass index               │   25.69      26.20          0.51         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²    d     n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2.13       5.64     <.001  0.02  0.25  1200  #>  Body mass index               │   0.09       0.93      .018  0.00  0.14  1188   # Hedges' g with weighted analysis and weighted n column. table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   weights = weight,   statistic = TRUE,   effect_size = "g",   show_weighted_n = TRUE ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   67.01      70.88          3.87         #>  Body mass index               │   25.51      25.98          0.47         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL   t      p     R²    g    #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2.11       5.62     4.33  <.001  0.02  0.25  #>  Body mass index               │   0.05       0.89     2.18   .030  0.00  0.13  #>  #>  Variable                      │  n    Weighted n  #> ───────────────────────────────┼────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 1200   1196.47    #>  Body mass index               │ 1188   1183.32     # Hedges' g with noncentral t confidence interval (bracket notation). table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   effect_size = "g",   effect_size_ci = TRUE ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   67.16      71.05          3.89         #>  Body mass index               │   25.69      26.20          0.51         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²   #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2.13       5.64     <.001  0.02  #>  Body mass index               │   0.09       0.93      .018  0.00  #>  #>  Variable                      │         g           n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 0.25 [0.14, 0.36]  1200  #>  Body mass index               │ 0.14 [0.02, 0.25]  1188   # Cohen's \eqn{f^2}{f^2} alongside \eqn{R^2}{R^2} (familiar power-analysis effect size). table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   effect_size = "f2" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   67.16      71.05          3.89         #>  Body mass index               │   25.69      26.20          0.51         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²    f²    n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2.13       5.64     <.001  0.02  0.02  1200  #>  Body mass index               │   0.09       0.93      .018  0.00  0.00  1188   # Hays' omega-squared for a 3-level predictor (d / g would error here). table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = education,   effect_size = "omega2" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Highest education level #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Lower secondary)  M (Upper secondary)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │        67.68                81.56         #>  Body mass index               │        26.17                23.55         #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Tertiary)    p     R²    ω²    n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │    66.10      <.001  0.21  0.21  1200  #>  Body mass index               │    26.35      <.001  0.13  0.13  1188   # --- Robust SE for a numeric predictor ----------------------------------  # HC3 standard errors for the slope of a continuous predictor. table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = age,   vcov = "HC3",   ci = FALSE ) #> Continuous outcomes by Age (years) #>  #>  Variable                        │   B        p       R²      n     #> ─────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100)   │  0.04     .176    0.00    1200   #>  Body mass index                 │  0.04    <.001    0.02    1188    # Cluster-robust SE for repeated-measures data: the `sleep` dataset # has 10 subjects measured twice (one observation per group). table_continuous_lm(   sleep,   select = extra,   by = group,   cluster = ID,   vcov = "CR2" ) #> Registered S3 method overwritten by 'clubSandwich': #>   method    from     #>   bread.mlm sandwich #> Continuous outcomes by group #>  #>  Variable │ M (1)  M (2)  Δ (2 - 1)  95% CI LL  95% CI UL   p     R²   n   #> ──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  extra    │ 0.75   2.33     1.58       0.70       2.46     .003  0.16  20   # --- Covariate adjustment ----------------------------------------------  # Adjust the comparison of `wellbeing_score` and `bmi` by `sex` for `age` # and `education`. The footer surfaces the adjustment estimand # ("proportional" by default = G-computation, matching Stata `margins`). table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   covariates = c(age, education),   vcov = "HC3" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   67.27      70.93          3.65         #>  Body mass index               │   25.65      26.23          0.58         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²    n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2.08       5.23     <.001  0.22  1200  #>  Body mass index               │   0.19       0.97      .004  0.16  1188  #>  #> Note. Adjusted for age, education (proportional).  # Same model with the emmeans / SPSS UNIANOVA convention (equal-weight # marginal means on a synthetic covariate grid). table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   covariates = c(age, education),   adjustment = "balanced",   vcov = "HC3" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   69.93      73.59          3.65         #>  Body mass index               │   25.04      25.62          0.58         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²    n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2.08       5.23     <.001  0.22  1200  #>  Body mass index               │   0.19       0.97      .004  0.16  1188  #>  #> Note. Adjusted for age, education (balanced).  # Effect sizes adjust automatically: f2 / omega2 become partial # effect sizes via partial F (drop1) restricted to the focal `by`. # d / g are undefined under adjustment and raise spicy_unsupported. table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   covariates = c(age, education),   effect_size = "f2",   effect_size_ci = TRUE ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   67.27      70.93          3.65         #>  Body mass index               │   25.65      26.23          0.58         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²   #> ───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2.09       5.22     <.001  0.22  #>  Body mass index               │   0.19       0.97      .004  0.16  #>  #>  Variable                      │        f²           n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │ 0.02 [0.01, 0.04]  1200  #>  Body mass index               │ 0.01 [0.00, 0.02]  1188  #>  #> Note. Adjusted for age, education (proportional).  # --- Article-style polish -----------------------------------------------  # Pretty outcome labels and adjusted \eqn{R^2}{R^2}. table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   labels = c(     wellbeing_score = "WHO-5 wellbeing (0-100)",     bmi = "Body-mass index (kg/m^2)"   ),   r2 = "adj_r2" ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                 │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  95% CI LL  #> ──────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing (0-100)  │   67.16      71.05          3.89           2.13     #>  Body-mass index (kg/m^2) │   25.69      26.20          0.51           0.09     #>  #>  Variable                 │ 95% CI UL    p    Adj. R²   n    #> ──────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing (0-100)  │   5.64     <.001   0.01    1200  #>  Body-mass index (kg/m^2) │   0.93      .018   0.00    1188   # European decimal comma. table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   decimal_mark = "," ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                      │ M (Female)  M (Male)  Δ (Male - Female)  #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   67,16      71,05          3,89         #>  Body mass index               │   25,69      26,20          0,51         #>  #>  Variable                      │ 95% CI LL  95% CI UL    p     R²    n    #> ───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────── #>  WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100) │   2,13       5,64     <,001  0,02  1200  #>  Body mass index               │   0,09       0,93      ,018  0,00  1188   # Regex selection of all columns starting with "life_sat". table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = "^life_sat",   by = sex,   regex = TRUE ) #> Continuous outcomes by Sex #>  #>  Variable                                   │ M (Female)  M (Male)  #> ────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────── #>  Satisfaction with health (1-5)             │    3.51       3.59    #>  Satisfaction with work (1-5)               │    3.32       3.44    #>  Satisfaction with relationships (1-5)      │    3.71       3.74    #>  Satisfaction with standard of living (1-5) │    3.37       3.42    #>  #>  Variable                                   │ Δ (Male - Female)  95% CI LL  #> ────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────── #>  Satisfaction with health (1-5)             │       0.08           -0.06    #>  Satisfaction with work (1-5)               │       0.12           -0.01    #>  Satisfaction with relationships (1-5)      │       0.04           -0.09    #>  Satisfaction with standard of living (1-5) │       0.05           -0.08    #>  #>  Variable                                   │ 95% CI UL   p     R²    n    #> ────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────── #>  Satisfaction with health (1-5)             │   0.22     .267  0.00  1192  #>  Satisfaction with work (1-5)               │   0.26     .073  0.00  1192  #>  Satisfaction with relationships (1-5)      │   0.16     .570  0.00  1192  #>  Satisfaction with standard of living (1-5) │   0.18     .453  0.00  1192   # --- Output formats -----------------------------------------------------  # The rendered outputs below all wrap the same call: #   table_continuous_lm(sochealth, #                       select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi), #                       by = sex) # only `output` changes. Assign to a variable to avoid the # console-friendly text fallback that some engines fall back to # when printed directly in `?` help.  # Wide data.frame (one row per outcome). table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   output = "data.frame" ) #>                                      Variable M (Female) M (Male) #> wellbeing_score WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100)   67.16194 71.04879 #> bmi                           Body mass index   25.68506 26.19685 #>                 Δ (Male - Female)  95% CI LL 95% CI UL            p          R² #> wellbeing_score         3.8868576 2.12952733 5.6441879 1.548898e-05 0.015475137 #> bmi                     0.5117882 0.08879857 0.9347778 1.776254e-02 0.004728908 #>                    n #> wellbeing_score 1200 #> bmi             1188  # Raw long data.frame (one block per outcome). table_continuous_lm(   sochealth,   select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi),   by = sex,   output = "long" ) #>          variable                         label predictor_type predictor_label #> 1 wellbeing_score WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100)    categorical             Sex #> 2 wellbeing_score WHO-5 wellbeing index (0-100)    categorical             Sex #> 3             bmi               Body mass index    categorical             Sex #> 4             bmi               Body mass index    categorical             Sex #>    level reference estimate_type   emmean emmean_se emmean_ci_lower #> 1 Female    Female          <NA> 67.16194 0.6227155        65.94020 #> 2   Male    Female    difference 71.04879 0.6438305        69.78563 #> 3 Female    Female          <NA> 25.68506 0.1495988        25.39156 #> 4   Male    Female    difference 26.19685 0.1552460        25.89227 #>   emmean_ci_upper  estimate estimate_se estimate_ci_lower estimate_ci_upper #> 1        68.38367        NA          NA                NA                NA #> 2        72.31195 3.8868576   0.8957077        2.12952733         5.6441879 #> 3        25.97857        NA          NA                NA                NA #> 4        26.50144 0.5117882   0.2155948        0.08879857         0.9347778 #>   test_type statistic df1  df2      p.value es_type es_value es_ci_lower #> 1         F 18.830621   1 1198 1.548898e-05    <NA>       NA          NA #> 2         t  4.339426   1 1198 1.548898e-05    <NA>       NA          NA #> 3         F  5.635133   1 1186 1.776254e-02    <NA>       NA          NA #> 4         t  2.373843   1 1186 1.776254e-02    <NA>       NA          NA #>   es_ci_upper          r2      adj_r2    n weighted_n #> 1          NA 0.015475137 0.014653330 1200         NA #> 2          NA          NA          NA 1200         NA #> 3          NA 0.004728908 0.003889725 1188         NA #> 4          NA          NA          NA 1188         NA  # \donttest{ # Rendered HTML / docx objects -- best viewed inside a # Quarto / R Markdown document or a pkgdown article. if (requireNamespace("tinytable", quietly = TRUE)) {   tt <- table_continuous_lm(     sochealth, select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi), by = sex,     output = "tinytable"   ) } if (requireNamespace("gt", quietly = TRUE)) {   tbl <- table_continuous_lm(     sochealth, select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi), by = sex,     output = "gt"   ) } if (requireNamespace("flextable", quietly = TRUE)) {   ft <- table_continuous_lm(     sochealth, select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi), by = sex,     output = "flextable"   ) }  # Excel and Word: write to a temporary file. if (requireNamespace("openxlsx2", quietly = TRUE)) {   tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".xlsx")   table_continuous_lm(     sochealth, select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi), by = sex,     output = "excel", excel_path = tmp   )   unlink(tmp) } if (   requireNamespace("flextable", quietly = TRUE) &&     requireNamespace("officer", quietly = TRUE) ) {   tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".docx")   table_continuous_lm(     sochealth, select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi), by = sex,     output = "word", word_path = tmp   )   unlink(tmp) } # }  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Clipboard: writes to the system clipboard. table_continuous_lm(   sochealth, select = c(wellbeing_score, bmi), by = sex,   output = "clipboard" ) } # }

title: Regression coefficient summary table — table_regression
text: Publication-ready coefficient table one fitted lm / glm models. Supports standardised coefficients (\(\beta\)), average marginal effects (AME), partial effect sizes (\(f^2\) / \(\eta^2\) / \(\omega^2\) lm; partial \(\chi^2\) glm), pseudo-\(R^2\) (glm), full vocabulary variance estimators (classical / HC* / cluster-robust Satterthwaite-corrected df / bootstrap / jackknife). glm covers binomial / poisson / Gamma / inverse.gaussian / quasi families link.

title: Regression coefficient summary table — table_regression
code: table_regression(   models,   vcov = "classical",   cluster = NULL,   ci_level = 0.95,   ci_method = c("wald", "profile"),   boot_n = 1000L,   standardized = c("none", "refit", "posthoc", "basic", "smart", "pseudo"),   exponentiate = FALSE,   p_adjust = "none",   show_columns = NULL,   keep = NULL,   drop = NULL,   show_intercept = TRUE,   show_thresholds = TRUE,   intercept_position = c("first", "last"),   factor_layout = c("grouped", "flat"),   reference_style = c("row", "annotation", "footer", "none"),   reference_label = "(ref.)",   show_fit_stats = NULL,   fit_stats_layout = c("first_col", "merged"),   show_re = TRUE,   re_scale = c("sd", "variance"),   re_columns = c("est", "se", "ci"),   model_labels = NULL,   outcome_labels = NULL,   stars = FALSE,   nested = FALSE,   digits = 2L,   p_digits = 3L,   effect_size_digits = 2L,   fit_digits = 2L,   ic_digits = 1L,   decimal_mark = ".",   align = c("decimal", "center", "right"),   padding = 0L,   labels = NULL,   title = NULL,   note = NULL,   output = c("default", "data.frame", "long", "gt", "flextable", "tinytable", "excel",     "clipboard", "word"),   excel_path = NULL,   excel_sheet = "Regression",   clipboard_delim = "\t",   word_path = NULL,   word_template = NULL )

title: Regression coefficient summary table — table_regression
text: models fitted model object, list fits (named unnamed; classes may mixed). Single fits auto-promoted 1-element list. broad set model classes supported – linear / generalised linear (lm, glm, MASS::glm.nb), mixed-effects (lmer, lme, glmmTMB), survival (coxph, survreg), ordinal (polr, clm), mgcv::gam/bam, betareg, mlogit, survey::svyglm, rms (ols/lrm/cph/Glm), Bayesian (rstanarm/brms), among others. unsupported class raises spicy_unsupported_class. Raw data + formula accepted – fit-API. vcov Variance-covariance estimator: "classical", "HC0"-"HC5", "CR0"-"CR3", "bootstrap", "jackknife". scalar recycled models; list (one string per model) allows mixed estimators. Default "classical". See Inference standard errors. cluster Cluster identifier cluster-robust variance (used vcov "CR0"-"CR3" cluster-bootstrap / cluster-jackknife). Three accepted forms (see specify cluster details): Formula: ~region, ~region:year (recommended). String column name: "region". Atomic vector length nobs(fit): df$region, interaction(df$region, df$year), ... (keys derived fly). multi-model use, pass list one form per model (mix--match allowed). Bare unquoted names (cluster = region) accepted – use ~region "region". Default NULL (clustering). ci_level Confidence level reported CIs (B, \(\beta\), AME, partial effect sizes). Default 0.95. ci_method CI construction. "wald" (default) uses estimate +/- z x SE (t x SE lm). "profile" (glm ordinal MASS::polr / ordinal::clm) uses profile-likelihood CI stats::confint() – MASS::confint.glm() glm, confint.polr() / confint.clm() ordinal fits – asymmetric, exact likelihood-based inference (Venables & Ripley MASS Section 7.2). CI bounds change; estimate, SE, statistic p-value remain Wald. ordinal fits profile covers predictor coefficients (cut-point thresholds stay Wald), robust vcov takes precedence (Wald-robust CIs used instead). "profile" lm raises spicy_invalid_input. boot_n Number bootstrap replicates vcov = "bootstrap". Single positive integer. Default 1000L. standardized Standardisation method "beta" column. One "none" (default), "refit", "posthoc", "basic", "smart", "pseudo". "pseudo" glm (Menard 2011 fully-standardised); using lm() raises spicy_invalid_input. See Standardised coefficients section. exponentiate Logical. TRUE model glm non-identity link, B, CI bounds, SE transformed via exp() (delta method: SE_OR = x SE_log-odds). column header rebranded per family / link: (binomial logit), IRR (poisson log), HR (binomial cloglog), RR (binomial log), MR (Gamma log), else exp(B). statistic p-value stay link scale (invariant monotone transformation). Default FALSE. effect lm identity-link glm; emits spicy_ignored_arg warning cases. p_adjust Multiple-comparison adjustment method applied family estimated coefficient p-values within model (intercept reference rows excluded). One "none" (default), "holm", "hochberg", "hommel", "bonferroni", "BH" / "fdr", "". Delegated stats::p.adjust(); applied per-model per estimate_type (B AME p-values adjusted independently within families). Active adjustments documented footer (method + family size). See Multiple-comparison adjustment section appropriate. show_columns Character vector tokens selecting per-coefficient columns display order. Accepts atomic tokens ("b", "se", "ci", "t", "p", "beta", "ame", "ame_se", "ame_ci", "ame_p", "partial_f2" + "partial_f2_ci", "partial_eta2" + "partial_eta2_ci", "partial_omega2" + "partial_omega2_ci", "partial_chi2") group tokens ("all_b", "all_b_compact", "all_b_full", "all_beta", "all_ame", "all_ame_compact", "all_f2", "all_eta2", "all_omega2"). See Vocabulary tokens details full enumeration. Default NULL selects context-aware layout: "all_b" (single model) "all_b_compact" (multi- model). "p" token always B / beta p-value; AME-specific p-value use "ame_p". keep Character vector regexes. coefficient rows whose term name (stats::coef() – e.g. "wt", "cyl6", "factor(cyl)8") matches least one pattern kept. Mutually exclusive drop. Filtering display choice; p_adjust runs full coefficient family filtering. Default NULL (filter). drop Character vector regexes. Coefficient rows matching pattern removed. Mutually exclusive keep. Default NULL. show_intercept Whether display intercept row. Default TRUE (APA convention). Hide via FALSE. show_thresholds ordinal cumulative-link models (MASS::polr, ordinal::clm), whether display estimated category thresholds (cut-points) subordinate "Thresholds" block rows predictors, carrying B / SE / CI / p like predictor rows. Default TRUE. FALSE collapses compact one-line footer note instead. Thresholds reported log-odds (B) scale never exponentiated (exponentiate = TRUE rows stay log-odds scale). effect non-ordinal models, rows shown coefficient column ("b"/"beta") show_columns. intercept_position place intercept shown. "first" (default, APA) "last" (Stata-style, intercept just fit-stats footer). Ignored show_intercept = FALSE (spicy_ignored_arg warning). factor_layout Layout factor predictors. Applies categorical predictor – factor, ordered, character, logical (R coerces latter two factors fit time). Two options: "grouped" (default): variable name header row ending : (e.g., education:); level follows indented sub-row bare level name. APA convention. "flat": non-reference dummy one row <variable><level> form (e.g., educationUpper); header, indent. Econometrics convention. reference_style Rendering factor reference levels. Four modes, distinguishing reference information exposed (row, inline, footer, nowhere): "row" (default): explicit row Female (ref.) em-dashes stat columns (NEJM / BMJ clinical convention). reference_label controls suffix. "annotation": row dropped reference shown inline. factor_layout = "grouped" factor header reads education: [ref: Lower]; factor_layout = "flat" marker [vs Lower] attached first non-reference dummy factor (subsequent dummies inherit reference). "footer": row dropped single line Reference categories: education = Lower; sex = Female. added footer note. SAS PROC LOGISTIC / SPSS "Categorical Variables Codings" convention. Best publication-grade dense multi-factor tables. "none": row dropped reference information displayed anywhere. user responsible stating reference convention elsewhere (article text, table caption). factor_layout = "flat", informational message emitted flag silent omission. Ordered factors AME: R's default contr.poly, ordered factors B coefficients named .L / .Q / .C (orthogonal polynomial trends) per-level reference semantics. "ame" show_columns, however, AME block per-level contrasts levels()[1]. synthetic reference row anchored levels()[1] therefore emitted reader sees AME baseline explicitly, reference_style handling plain treatment-coded factors. [vs <ref>] annotation "annotation" mode attached first AME row, polynomial-trend rows. reference_label Suffix shown reference level reference_style = "row" mode. Default "(ref.)". Ignored three modes (use structural English wording – "ref:", "vs", "Reference categories:"). show_fit_stats Character vector tokens model-level rows coefficients; row order follows token order. NULL (default) resolves class-aware: lm: c("nobs", "r2", "adj_r2"). glm, ordinal polr / clm: c("nobs", "pseudo_r2_mcfadden", "pseudo_r2_nagelkerke", "AIC") (McFadden = Stata ologit default, Nagelkerke = SPSS PLUM). mixed lm + glm: union two (renderer em-dashes per cell stat defined given model class). nested = TRUE default extended class-appropriate change-stat tokens (e.g. "r2_change", "f_change" lm). See Vocabulary tokens (show_fit_stats subsection) Hierarchical (nested) model comparison details full vocabulary. fit_stats_layout Layout fit-stat values (n, R^2, AIC, ...) within model's column group. Two options: "first_col" (default): value placed FIRST numeric sub-column model (typically B); model's remaining sub-columns (SE, LL, UL, p, ...) left empty row. APA Manual 7 Table 7.13 layout. "merged": model's numeric sub-columns merged single wide cell containing fit-stat value, centred model spanner. Stata esttab layout / Econometrica AER journal convention. Resolves mixed-precision look "first_col" (integer n row sharing B column two-decimal coefficients). Cell merging supported excel, flextable, word (via flextable). gt, tinytable, clipboard, default (console) always render "first_col" mode regardless setting: gt lacks native row-spanning cell-merge API (tab_spanner covers columns, row-cell ranges). tinytable's style_tt(colspan = N) emits HTML colspan header rows, body cells. clipboard ships TSV plaintext. default ships fixed-width ASCII. Decimal alignment every numeric column preserved modes: B column decimal-aligns coefficient values plus fit-stat value(s) "first_col" mode (native primitives handle mixed-precision case), trivially decimal-aligns "merged" mode (fit-stat values move B column merged cell). show_re Logical. TRUE (default) prints random-effects panel fit-statistics footer mixed-effects fits (lmer, glmer, glmmTMB, lme). FALSE suppresses panel entirely. effect fits without random effects (lm, glm, coxph, ...). panel header carries estimator label ((REML) (ML)); see Mixed-effects models section vignette("table-regression") methodological rationale (Gelman 2005; Bates et al. 2015; Bolker FAQ). re_scale One "sd" (default) "variance". Controls display scale random-effects panel: "sd": report random-effect standard deviation \(\sigma\) (Gelman 2005, Technometrics: "directly interpretable size variation across groups"). Standard error CI converted via Delta method: \(SE(\sigma) = SE(\sigma^2) / (2\sigma)\); \(CI(\sigma) = \sqrt{CI(\sigma^2)}\). "variance": report \(\sigma^2\) (canonical internal scale; SE CI come straight Hessian / nlme::intervals() / glmmTMB::confint() without rescaling). Correlation rows (\(\rho\)) unitless pass either way. re_columns Character vector. Subset c("est", "se", "ci") controlling columns random-effects panel rendered. "est" mandatory. Useful slimming output (re_columns = "est") journals want standard errors (re_columns = c("est", "se")). Note. Standard errors CIs Wald (est ± z * SE, clamped 0 variances). Wald can optimistic near variance boundary (Self & Liang 1987 chi-bar-squared); profile-likelihood intervals available directly fitted model (confint(fit, method = "profile") lmer) robustness critical. See Mixed-effects models section vignette("table-regression"). model_labels Per-model labels used column-group spanner model's sub-columns (console + gt / flextable / tinytable / Excel / Word renderers). NULL (default) resolves automatically; see Multi-model semantics full rule. character vector length length(models) overrides. outcome_labels Optional Outcome body row override. NULL (default) hides row entirely – multi-model spanner DV already visible data. character vector length length(models) forces explicit Outcome row values (spanner stays "Model 1, ..." unless model_labels also supplied). FALSE also suppresses row. stars Significance asterisks. FALSE (default, APA 7 Section 6.46) – stars. TRUE – APA cutoffs c("*" = 0.05, "**" = 0.01, "***" = 0.001). named numeric vector specifies custom thresholds, e.g. c("+" = 0.10, "*" = 0.05, "**" = 0.01, "***" = 0.001). nested Whether inject pairwise change-statistic rows adjacent models (M2 vs M1, M3 vs M2, ...). FALSE (default) – pure side--side display. TRUE – requires identical nobs identical response variable across models. See Hierarchical (nested) model comparison. digits Decimal places general numeric tokens (b, beta, se, ci, t, f_change, lrt_change, deviance, deviance_change, ame, ame_se, weighted_nobs). Default 2L. p_digits Decimal places p-values (p, ame_p, p_change). APA-strict: leading zero stripped, <.001 (<.0001 etc. depending p_digits) small values. Default 3L. effect_size_digits Decimals per-coefficient effect sizes (partial_f2, partial_eta2, partial_omega2). Default 2L. fit_digits Decimals variance-explained / model-level effect-size fit stats (r2, adj_r2, r2_change, adj_r2_change, omega2, f2, f2_change, sigma, rmse). Default 2L. ic_digits Decimals information criteria (AIC, AICc, BIC, _change form). Default 1L. decimal_mark Decimal mark used numeric display. "." (default) "," (European convention). "," used, CI bracket separator switches "; " automatically avoid "0,18 [0,07, 0,30]" ambiguity. align Numeric column alignment. "decimal" (default) – pre-pad cells decimal marks line vertically (publication-style). CI cells ([LL, UL]) left bracket, LL decimal point, comma separator, UL decimal point, right bracket independently aligned across rows. "center", "right", "auto" legacy per-column alignment. padding Non-negative integer giving extra characters added data column's auto-computed width default print method renders table. Default 0L (compact – fits models console / page width). Use 2L (Stata-like) 4L spacious look. Headers stay centered data region regardless padding. labels Named character vector overriding per-coefficient row labels. Names coefficient term names (stats::terms()); values displayed labels. E.g. c("age" = "Age (years)", "sexM" = "Male (vs Female)"). Default NULL (use raw term names). title, note Override suppress auto-built caption / methodological footer. Three modes per argument: NULL (default): package builds standard caption ("Linear regression <DV>" / "Hierarchical linear regression <DV>" / ...) methodological note (VCV type, p-adjust method, reference categories, ...). FALSE: corresponding banner row omitted every output engine. Use surrounding manuscript provides caption / note. character string (length 1): replaces auto-built text verbatim. renderer applies APA formatting top – supply exact string want displayed (multi- line notes accepted via embedded "\n"). Validation messages, spanner row, -body change- stat rows affected – belong table structure, banner. output Output type. "default" (printable spicy_regression_table); "data.frame" / "long" (raw data); "gt" / "flextable" / "tinytable" (rich-format tables); "excel" (writes excel_path); "clipboard" (copies system clipboard); "word" (writes flextable word_path). excel_path File path output = "excel". Default NULL (required output = "excel"). excel_sheet Sheet name writing Excel. Default "Regression". clipboard_delim Field delimiter output = "clipboard". Default "\t" (tab-separated, pastes cleanly Excel / Google Sheets / Word). clipboard payload mirrors Excel layout (title row, spanner row, header, body, footer note) plain text – horizontal rules, cell merging, decimal alignment, monospace font, factor-level indentation encoded TSV therefore absent paste. Paste behaviour target: Excel / Google Sheets: numerics auto-detected right-aligned; text cells stay left-aligned. (P-values .005 get re-parsed 0.005 Excel's auto-format – preserve APA leading-zero-dropped display, prefer output = "excel".) Word: paste converted Word table; cells start left-aligned. Apply Table Style (Insert > Table > Design) APA-style borders, set right-alignment numeric columns (Layout > Align Right). self-contained Word file borders alignment pre-applied, use output = "word" instead. word_path File path output = "word". Default NULL (required output = "word"). Word table inherits flextable styling (Calibri font, APA borders, decimal-aligned numerics) adds Word-specific features: auto-numbered caption ("Table 1: ...", "Table 2: ...") via Word's SEQ field multiple table_regression() calls one document number consecutively; re-printed header row page break; row split prevention single coefficient row never wraps across two pages; APA-styled note line (*Note.* italic prefix per APA Manual 7 §7.14). R Markdown / Quarto: embedded use, prefer output = "flextable" (returns flextable object knits docx/HTML/PDF natively). output = "word" writes standalone .docx file, suited scripted exports rather chunk-level rendering. word_template Optional path custom .docx file used template output = "word". template's header, footer, page size, margins, named styles ("Table Caption" particular) honoured; table appended template body. Useful institutional templates pre-set headers ("APA Style", "Manuscript Submission Template", etc.). Default NULL (uses flextable's stock template). Customising caption appearance: table caption tagged Word named style "Table Caption". visual rendering (italic / bold / colour / font) follows whatever style set docx template. stock Word template renders "Table Caption" italic — APA Manual 7 §7.10 condensed convention. different appearance (Nature-style bold non-italic, APA-strict 2-line bold-number / italic-title, etc.), edit "Table Caption" style docx template pass via word_template = "your_template.docx". Style-based delegation keeps rendered caption consistent surrounding document lets editorial conventions (Nature, APA-strict, journal-specific) applied without modifying call site.

title: Regression coefficient summary table — table_regression
text: spicy_regression_table object (data.frame subclass classes c("spicy_regression_table", "spicy_table", "data.frame")) output = "default". result carries rendering attributes (title, note, align, padding) provenance attributes (outcome, model_ids) consumed print method broom methods. output values, returns format-specific object (gt_tbl, flextable, tinytable, data.frame, tbl_df, invisible(x) side-effect outputs).

title: Regression coefficient summary table — table_regression
text: Two vector arguments – show_columns show_fit_stats – accept named tokens select display order. tokens lowercase (snake_case compound tokens). Group tokens ("all_b", "all_ame", ...) expand fixed vector atomic tokens; see show_columns .

title: Regression coefficient summary table — table_regression
text: token = one displayed column. Coefficient family: "b", "beta" (standardised), "se", "ci", "t", "p". Marginal effects: "ame", "ame_se", "ame_ci", "ame_p". "p" always refers B-coefficient p-value; AME-specific p-value use "ame_p". Partial effect sizes – lm : "partial_f2", "partial_eta2", "partial_omega2", paired _ci companion ("partial_f2_ci", ...). Partial effect size – glm : "partial_chi2" (likelihood-ratio chi-square via drop1(test = "LRT"); SAS PROC LOGISTIC TYPE3; Long & Freese 2014 Section 3.5). Rendered value (df) disambiguate factor terms (k-1 df) numeric terms (1 df). Group tokens (presets) expand fixed atomic vector validation: "all_b"         -> c("b", "se", "ci", "p") "all_b_compact" -> c("b", "se", "p") "all_b_full"    -> c("b", "se", "ci", "t", "p") "all_beta"      -> c("b", "beta", "se", "ci", "p") "all_ame"       -> c("ame", "ame_se", "ame_ci", "ame_p") "all_ame_compact" -> c("ame", "ame_p") "all_f2" / "all_eta2" / "all_omega2" -> partial_* + _ci companion. Mix groups atomic tokens: show_columns = c("all_b", "ame", "ame_p"). Duplicates expansion deduplicated; order tokens controls order displayed columns. standardized != "none" "beta" already requested, auto-injected "b". Asking "beta" standardized = "none" raises spicy_invalid_input. Default (show_columns = NULL) context-aware: "all_b" single model (APA-7 Section 6.46 publication layout), "all_b_compact" two models (CI dropped fit side--side layout; restore explicitly needed).

what: show_fit_stats – model-level rows below the coefficients
title: Regression coefficient summary table — table_regression
text: Counts: "nobs", "weighted_nobs". Variance explained (lm ): "r2", "adj_r2", "omega2". Pseudo-\(R^2\) (glm ordinal polr / clm): "pseudo_r2_mcfadden" (McFadden 1974), "pseudo_r2_nagelkerke" (Nagelkerke 1991), "pseudo_r2_tjur" (Tjur 2009; binomial ). Residual scale: "sigma" (lm \(\hat{\sigma}\) / glm dispersion), "rmse". Effect size: "f2". Information criteria: "AIC", "AICc", "BIC", "deviance". Change-stats hierarchical comparison (active nested = TRUE; see Hierarchical comparison ): "r2_change", "adj_r2_change", "f_change", "f2_change", "lrt_change", "aic_change", "aicc_change", "bic_change", "deviance_change", "p_change". Default (resolved NULL) class-aware: lm fits get c("nobs", "r2", "adj_r2"); glm ordinal polr / clm fits get c("nobs", "pseudo_r2_mcfadden", "pseudo_r2_nagelkerke", "AIC"); mixed lm + glm sets union groups (renderer per-row em-dashes inappropriate cell). nested = TRUE, class-aware default extended change tokens (c("r2_change", "f_change", "p_change") lm, c("lrt_change", "p_change") glm). order tokens show_fit_stats controls order rows.

title: Regression coefficient summary table — table_regression
text: Pass single fit list() fits. Multi-model layout draws centred spanner label model's sub-columns: list("Naive" = m1, "Adjusted" = m2) -> spanner labels "Naive" / "Adjusted". Partial naming (list("Naive" = m1, m2)) auto-fills missing slots "Model <position>". list(m1, m2) (unnamed) -> response variables differ, bare DV name (formula(fit)[[2]]) becomes spanner label redundant Outcome body row suppressed. DVs match, labels default "Model 1, 2, ...". model_labels = c("", "B") overrides everything. Duplicate explicit names list rejected (spicy_invalid_input) – silently collide internal model_id key.

title: Regression coefficient summary table — table_regression
text: vcov selects variance-covariance estimator: "classical" – OLS (lm) / Fisher information (glm). "HC0" "HC5" – heteroskedasticity-consistent (via sandwich::vcovHC()). "CR0" "CR3" – cluster-robust Satterthwaite-corrected df (via clubSandwich::vcovCR()). Requires cluster. "bootstrap" – nonparametric cluster bootstrap (boot_n replicates). "jackknife" – leave-one-/ leave-one-cluster-. multi-model use, vcov cluster accept single value (recycled models) list (one per model). fit can appear several times different estimators compare standard errors side--side. Inferential regimes lm / glm (B AME share regime): classical, HC* -> t df.residual (lm) / z-asymptotic (glm). bootstrap, jackknife -> z asymptotic. CR0-CR3 -> t Satterthwaite-corrected df (B via clubSandwich::coef_test(); AME via clubSandwich::linear_contrast(); Pustejovsky & Tipton 2018). non-linear terms (poly(), (), log(), splines::ns()), AME falls back z-asymptotic spicy_fallback warning. classes t-vs-z axis follows estimator's native reference distribution (e.g. glm, glmmTMB, survival, ordinal, betareg, mlogit use z; lm, lme, lmer, rms::ols use t).

what: Robust SE availability by model class
title: Regression coefficient summary table — table_regression
text: every estimator defined every class. robust vcov class honour fails fast spicy_unsupported_vcov – never silent model-based result robust label: lm, glm, MASS::glm.nb classical, HC*, CR*, bootstrap, jackknife. mlogit classical, HC*, CR* (cluster choice-situation level). lmer, lme, glmmTMB, coxph, survreg, mgcv::gam/bam, polr, clm, betareg, survey::svyglm, rms (ols/lrm/cph/Glm) classical + CR* – HC* resamplers (refit lm/glm) defined . clm scale / nominal (partial-PO) component classical . classes (glmer, nnet::multinom, rstanarm/brms, ...) classical (model-based) . Cluster-robust backends differ class cross-validated field-standard oracle: lm/glm/lmer/lme/glmmTMB use clubSandwich (CR2 = Bell-McCaffrey, Satterthwaite df lm/lme/lmer); coxph/cph use Lin-Wei grouped-dfbeta sandwich (identical coxph(..., cluster=)); survreg/gam/polr/clm/betareg/mlogit use sandwich::vcovCL(); svyglm uses design-aware clubSandwich estimator; rms fits use rms::robcov() (needs fit's x = TRUE, y = TRUE). single cluster sandwiches CR0-CR3 bias-reduction variants, requested CR* maps one available estimator. cluster length one entry per observation, except mlogit (one per choice situation) censored coxph (one per subject).

title: Regression coefficient summary table — table_regression
text: Three accepted forms, order preference: Formula – cluster = ~region (cluster = ~region:year interaction two variables). variables looked model.frame(fit) first, original data argument captured fit. Recommended: independent dataset's name, composable multi-way clustering, consistent sandwich::vcovCL() / clubSandwich::vcovCR(). String – cluster = "region". single column name resolved way formula. Convenient express interactions. Vector – cluster = df$region. atomic vector length nobs(fit). Use cluster key derived fly (cluster = interaction(df$region, df$year), cluster = .integer(format(df$date, "%Y"))), comes different dataset matching row order, otherwise column model's data. Bare unquoted names (cluster = region) accepted – require non-standard evaluation magic breaks programmatic use (function wrapping, dynamic column choice, loops). Use ~region "region" instead. multi-model use, mix forms freely: cluster = list(~region, "region", df$region).

title: Regression coefficient summary table — table_regression
text: nested = TRUE adds per-pair change statistics -table rows (APA Table 7.13 / Stata esttab / SPSS Model Summary convention). adjacent pair (M2 vs M1, M3 vs M2, ...) contributes one column change stats; FIRST model column gets em-dashes (previous model compare ). Validation requires identical nobs identical response variable across models. Default change tokens auto-injected show_fit_stats NULL: -lm: c("r2_change", "f_change", "p_change") – APA hierarchical regression standard. -glm: c("lrt_change", "p_change") – Hosmer & Lemeshow Section 3.5; Long & Freese 2014 Section 3.6. customise, pass change tokens directly show_fit_stats. Variance-explained change tokens -glm hierarchy raise spicy_invalid_input (residual-sum--squares partition apply outside least-squares framework – renderer points user lrt_change).

title: Regression coefficient summary table — table_regression
text: standardized controls method "beta" show_columns: "refit" – refit z-scored data. lm X Y z-scored (Cohen et al. 2003 gold standard); glm numeric X (Long & Freese 2014 Section 4.3.4 "x-standardization"). "posthoc" – post-hoc scaling. lm: \(\beta = B \times SD(X) / SD(Y)\); glm: X-\(\beta = B \times SD(X)\) (Y undefined link scale). "basic" – like "posthoc" factor dummies scaled column SD. "smart" – Gelman (2008): divide binary predictors 2 * SD instead SD. "pseudo" – glm . Menard (2004, 2011) fully-standardised \(\beta = B \times SD(X) / SD(Y^*)\), \(Y^*\) latent variable link scale \(SD(Y^*) = \sqrt{Var(\hat{\eta}) + Var_{link}}\) (\(\pi^2/3\) logit, 1 probit, \(\pi^2/6\) cloglog). Binomial families ; non-binomial returns NA spicy_caveat. "none" (default) – \(\beta\) computed. interactions transformed predictors ((), poly(), log(), splines::ns()), spicy_caveat warns standardised coefficients terms subtle interpret (Cohen et al. 2003 Section 7.7; Aiken & West 1991). caveat auto-documented footer.

title: Regression coefficient summary table — table_regression
text: Adjusting p-values coefficients single regression model standard convention. coefficient tests distinct hypothesis distinct predictor – situation multiple-testing procedures designed (Rothman 1990; Greenland 2017; APA Manual 7 Section 6.46; Harrell Regression Modeling Strategies Section 5.4; Gelman, Hill & Yajima 2012). Hence default p_adjust = "none". Adjustment appropriate : mass screening prior hypothesis (typically "BH" / FDR), pre-registered multi-endpoint confirmatory designs (typically "holm"), journal / SAP explicitly requests . adjustment runs keep / drop filtering, family model's full coefficient set (intercept reference rows excluded), displayed subset – filtering display choice must change inferential family.

title: Regression coefficient summary table — table_regression
text: output selects return type: "default" – spicy_regression_table (data.frame subclass) printed via spicy_print_table(). "data.frame" / "long" – raw data.frame / long-format tibble. "gt" / "flextable" / "tinytable" – rich-format HTML / Word / PDF tables (require corresponding Suggests package). "excel" – writes excel_path via openxlsx2::write_xlsx(). "word" – writes word_path via flextable::save_as_docx(). "clipboard" – copies system clipboard via clipr::write_clip(). broom::tidy() returns long tibble one row per (model_id, term, estimate_type) broom-canonical column names (estimate, std.error, conf.low, conf.high, statistic, p.value). broom::glance() returns one row per model model-level statistics; df.residual kept numeric cluster-robust Satterthwaite df preserved.

title: Regression coefficient summary table — table_regression
text: weights argument: weights property fit (extracted via stats::weights()). Pass fitting: lm(y ~ x, data = df, weights = w). downstream computations (vcov, AME, standardisation, weighted_nobs) extract automatically.

title: Regression coefficient summary table — table_regression
text: Output English. Override user-facing strings via reference_label, model_labels, outcome_labels, labels. title footer post-processable via attr(result, "title") attr(result, "note").

title: Regression coefficient summary table — table_regression
text: Every error warning emitted table_regression() carries classed condition programmatic dispatch via tryCatch() withCallingHandlers(). Errors inherit spicy_error (root); warnings spicy_warning. Specific leaves used function include spicy_invalid_input, spicy_invalid_data, spicy_unsupported, spicy_missing_pkg, spicy_missing_column, spicy_ignored_arg, spicy_caveat, spicy_fallback. See spicy full taxonomy.

(2019). Moving world beyond "p < 0.05". American Statistician, 73(sup1), 1-19.

title: Regression coefficient summary table — table_regression
code: # ---- Single-model usage ------------------------------------------ fit <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ age + sex + smoking, data = sochealth)  # Default APA layout: B / SE / 95% CI / p plus the n / R^2 / # Adj.R^2 fit-stats footer. Factor reference level is annotated # with `(ref.)` and shows an em-dash in the statistic columns. table_regression(fit) #> Linear regression: wellbeing_score #>  #>  Variable        │    B      SE       95% CI        p    #> ─────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)     │   65.20  1.66  [61.95, 68.45]  <.001  #>  age             │    0.05  0.03  [-0.01,  0.11]   .130  #>  sex:            │                                       #>    Female (ref.) │     –     –          –          –     #>    Male          │    3.86  0.91  [ 2.08,  5.63]  <.001  #>  smoking:        │                                       #>    No (ref.)     │     –     –          –          –     #>    Yes           │   -1.72  1.11  [-3.89,  0.45]   .121  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n               │ 1175                                  #>  R²              │    0.02                               #>  Adj.R²          │    0.02                               #>  #> Note. Linear regression. #> Std. errors: classical (OLS).  # Standardised coefficients (beta) injected next to B. Four # methods available; "refit" is the SPSS / Stata regress, beta # gold standard. table_regression(fit, standardized = "refit") #> Linear regression: wellbeing_score #>  #>  Variable        │    B       β     SE       95% CI        p    #> ─────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)     │   65.20  -0.10  1.66  [61.95, 68.45]  <.001  #>  age             │    0.05   0.04  0.03  [-0.01,  0.11]   .130  #>  sex:            │                                              #>    Female (ref.) │     –      –     –          –          –     #>    Male          │    3.86   0.25  0.91  [ 2.08,  5.63]  <.001  #>  smoking:        │                                              #>    No (ref.)     │     –      –     –          –          –     #>    Yes           │   -1.72  -0.11  1.11  [-3.89,  0.45]   .121  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n               │ 1175                                         #>  R²              │    0.02                                      #>  Adj.R²          │    0.02                                      #>  #> Note. Linear regression. #> Std. errors: classical (OLS). #> β = standardised coefficient.  # Custom column set: B + AME + AME-specific p-value. Note that # the `p` token always belongs to B, never to AME -- use the # explicit `ame_p` token for AME inference. table_regression(   fit,   show_columns = c("b", "p", "ame", "ame_ci", "ame_p") ) #> Linear regression: wellbeing_score #>  #>  Variable        │    B       p     AME      95% CI        p    #> ─────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)     │   65.20  <.001                               #>  age             │    0.05   .130   0.05  [-0.01, 0.11]   .130  #>  sex:            │                                              #>    Female (ref.) │     –     –       –          –         –     #>    Male          │    3.86  <.001   3.86  [ 2.08, 5.63]  <.001  #>  smoking:        │                                              #>    No (ref.)     │     –     –       –          –         –     #>    Yes           │   -1.72   .121  -1.72  [-3.89, 0.45]   .121  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n               │ 1175                                         #>  R²              │    0.02                                      #>  Adj.R²          │    0.02                                      #>  #> Note. Linear regression. #> Std. errors: classical (OLS). #> AME = average marginal effect.  # Group-token shortcut: "all_b" + "all_ame" expands to the full # B / AME column families side by side. table_regression(fit, show_columns = c("all_b", "all_ame")) #> Linear regression: wellbeing_score #>  #>  Variable        │    B      SE       95% CI        p     AME    SE   #> ─────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)     │   65.20  1.66  [61.95, 68.45]  <.001               #>  age             │    0.05  0.03  [-0.01,  0.11]   .130   0.05  0.03  #>  sex:            │                                                    #>    Female (ref.) │     –     –          –          –       –     –    #>    Male          │    3.86  0.91  [ 2.08,  5.63]  <.001   3.86  0.91  #>  smoking:        │                                                    #>    No (ref.)     │     –     –          –          –       –     –    #>    Yes           │   -1.72  1.11  [-3.89,  0.45]   .121  -1.72  1.11  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n               │ 1175                                               #>  R²              │    0.02                                            #>  Adj.R²          │    0.02                                            #>  #>  Variable        │    95% CI        p    #> ─────────────────┼────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)     │                       #>  age             │ [-0.01, 0.11]   .130  #>  sex:            │                       #>    Female (ref.) │       –         –     #>    Male          │ [ 2.08, 5.63]  <.001  #>  smoking:        │                       #>    No (ref.)     │       –         –     #>    Yes           │ [-3.89, 0.45]   .121  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n               │                       #>  R²              │                       #>  Adj.R²          │                       #>  #> Note. Linear regression. #> Std. errors: classical (OLS). #> AME = average marginal effect.  # ---- Cluster-robust variance ------------------------------------- # CR2 (Bell-McCaffrey) with Satterthwaite-corrected df is the # recommended default under few clusters. Three forms are accepted # for `cluster`; the formula is preferred for composability with # multi-way clustering and for programmatic robustness. table_regression(fit, vcov = "CR2", cluster = ~region) #> Linear regression: wellbeing_score #>  #>  Variable        │    B      SE       95% CI        p    #> ─────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)     │   65.20  1.76  [60.63, 69.78]  <.001  #>  age             │    0.05  0.04  [-0.05,  0.15]   .285  #>  sex:            │                                       #>    Female (ref.) │     –     –          –          –     #>    Male          │    3.86  0.85  [ 1.66,  6.05]   .007  #>  smoking:        │                                       #>    No (ref.)     │     –     –          –          –     #>    Yes           │   -1.72  1.52  [-5.70,  2.27]   .313  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n               │ 1175                                  #>  R²              │    0.02                               #>  Adj.R²          │    0.02                               #>  #> Note. Linear regression. #> Std. errors: cluster-robust (CR2), clusters by region. table_regression(fit, vcov = "CR2", cluster = "region") #> Linear regression: wellbeing_score #>  #>  Variable        │    B      SE       95% CI        p    #> ─────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)     │   65.20  1.76  [60.63, 69.78]  <.001  #>  age             │    0.05  0.04  [-0.05,  0.15]   .285  #>  sex:            │                                       #>    Female (ref.) │     –     –          –          –     #>    Male          │    3.86  0.85  [ 1.66,  6.05]   .007  #>  smoking:        │                                       #>    No (ref.)     │     –     –          –          –     #>    Yes           │   -1.72  1.52  [-5.70,  2.27]   .313  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n               │ 1175                                  #>  R²              │    0.02                               #>  Adj.R²          │    0.02                               #>  #> Note. Linear regression. #> Std. errors: cluster-robust (CR2), clusters by region. table_regression(fit, vcov = "CR2", cluster = ~region:age_group) #> Linear regression: wellbeing_score #>  #>  Variable        │    B      SE       95% CI        p    #> ─────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)     │   65.20  1.54  [61.91, 68.49]  <.001  #>  age             │    0.05  0.03  [-0.01,  0.10]   .107  #>  sex:            │                                       #>    Female (ref.) │     –     –          –          –     #>    Male          │    3.86  0.87  [ 2.05,  5.67]  <.001  #>  smoking:        │                                       #>    No (ref.)     │     –     –          –          –     #>    Yes           │   -1.72  1.25  [-4.31,  0.88]   .183  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n               │ 1175                                  #>  R²              │    0.02                               #>  Adj.R²          │    0.02                               #>  #> Note. Linear regression. #> Std. errors: cluster-robust (CR2), clusters by region:age_group.  # ---- Hierarchical (nested) regression ---------------------------- # Adds in-table change-statistic rows (Delta R^2 / F-change / # p-change for lm; LRT / p-change for glm) below the fit-stats. # Note: hierarchical comparison requires identical observations # across all models -- prepare a complete-case subset first so # R's listwise deletion does not produce different `nobs` per # model (which the function rejects). sochealth_cc <- na.omit(   sochealth[, c("wellbeing_score", "age", "sex", "smoking")] ) m1 <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ age,                  data = sochealth_cc) m2 <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ age + sex,            data = sochealth_cc) m3 <- lm(wellbeing_score ~ age + sex + smoking,  data = sochealth_cc) table_regression(   list("Step 1" = m1, "Step 2" = m2, "Step 3" = m3),   nested = TRUE ) #> Hierarchical linear regression: wellbeing_score #>  #>                           Step 1                Step 2              Step 3      #>                    ────────────────────  ─────────────────────  ──────────────  #>  Variable        │    B      SE     p       B       SE     p       B       SE   #> ─────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)     │   66.85  1.59  <.001    64.90   1.65  <.001    65.20   1.66  #>  age –      –     –         –      –    #>    Male          │                          3.87   0.91  <.001     3.86   0.91  #>  smoking:        │                                                              #>    No (ref.)     │                                                  –      –    #>    Yes           │                                                -1.72   1.11  #> +0.00         #>  F-change        │     –                  +18.26                  +2.41         #>  p (change)      │     –                    <.001                   .121        #>  #> (ref.) │  –     #>    Male          │ <.001  #>  smoking:        │        #>    No (ref.)     │  –     #>    Yes           │  .121  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n               │        #>  R² Linear regression models. #> Std. errors: classical (OLS).  # ---- Side-by-side variance comparison ---------------------------- # Same fit, three vcovs in one wide table. Useful for showing the # sensitivity of inference to the variance assumption. table_regression(   list("Classical" = fit, "HC3" = fit, "CR2" = fit),   vcov    = list("classical", "HC3", "CR2"),   cluster = list(NULL, NULL, ~region) ) #> Linear regression comparison: wellbeing_score #>  #>                         Classical                HC3                CR2       #> Linear regression models. #> Std. errors: #>   Model 1: classical (OLS) #>   Model 2: heteroskedasticity-robust (HC3) #>   Model 3: cluster-robust (CR2), clusters by region  # ---- Tidy long format for downstream pipelines ------------------- broom::tidy(table_regression(fit)) #> # A tibble: 4 × 15 #>   model_id outcome     term  estimate_type estimate std.error conf.low conf.high #>   <chr>    <chr>       <chr> <chr>            <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 M1       wellbeing_… (Int… B              65.2       1.66    62.0       68.5   #> 2 M1       wellbeing_… age   B               0.0465    0.0307  -0.0137     0.107 #> 3 M1       wellbeing_… sexM… B               3.86      0.905    2.08       5.63  #> 4 M1       wellbeing_… smok… B              -1.72      1.11    -3.89       0.454 #> # ℹ 7 more variables: statistic <dbl>, df <dbl>, p.value <dbl>, #> #   test_type <chr>, is_intercept <lgl>, factor_term <chr>, factor_level <chr>  # ---- Mixed-effects models ---------------------------------------- # Linear mixed-effects (lme4). The footer adds a random-effects # panel with sigma + Wald SE / CI from `merDeriv`, the Nakagawa # marginal / conditional R^2 fit-stats, and a per-class p-value # annotation line. if (requireNamespace("lme4", quietly = TRUE)) {   fit <- lme4::lmer(Reaction ~ Days + (Days | Subject),                      data = lme4::sleepstudy)   table_regression(fit)    # Switch to the variance scale (sigma^2 instead of sigma).   table_regression(fit, re_scale = "variance")    # Minimal random-effects display: estimates only, no SE / CI.   table_regression(fit, re_columns = "est")    # Suppress the random-effects panel entirely.   table_regression(fit, show_re = FALSE) } #> Registered S3 method overwritten by 'merDeriv': #>   method        from         #>   bread.lmerMod clubSandwich #> Linear mixed-effects regression: Reaction #>  #>  Variable         │    B      SE        95% CI         p    #> ──────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────── #>  (Intercept)      │  251.41  6.82  [238.03, 264.78]  <.001  #>  Days             │   10.47  1.55  [  7.44,  13.50]  <.001  #> ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌┼╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌ #>  n                │  180                                    #>  R² (marginal)    │    0.28                                 #>  R² (conditional) │    0.80                                 #>  AIC              │ 1755.6                                  #>  BIC              │ 1774.8                                  #>  #> Note. Linear mixed-effects regression. #> Std. errors: Wald (model-based). #> p-values: Wald-z, large-sample approximation. Load `lmerTest` for Satterthwaite t-tests.  # Hierarchical mixed-effects comparison (nested LRT). if (requireNamespace("lme4", quietly = TRUE)) {   m1 <- lme4::lmer(Reaction ~ 1     + (1 | Subject), Days  + (Days | Subject),                     data = lme4::sleepstudy, REML = FALSE)   table_regression(list(m1, m2, m3), nested = TRUE) } #> Hierarchical linear mixed-effects regression: Reaction #> title: Tidying methods for a spicy_categorical_table — tidy.spicy_categorical_table
text: tidy() returns one row per (variable x level) – per (variable x level x group) used – broom-conventional columns: outcome, level, group (applicable), n, proportion (percentage divided 100). glance() returns one row per outcome omnibus chi-squared test (used) requested association measure: outcome, test_type ("chi_squared"), statistic (chi-squared), df, p.value, assoc_type, assoc_value, assoc_ci_lower, assoc_ci_upper, n_total. Without , outcome n_total populated; columns NA.

title: Tidying methods for a spicy_continuous_lm_table — tidy.spicy_continuous_lm_table
text: tidy() returns one row per estimated parameter across outcomes: One row per fitted level mean (estimate_type = "emmean") categorical predictors, level name term. One row per contrast (estimate_type = "difference") binary contrast shown, term = "<level2> - <level1>". One row per slope (estimate_type = "slope") numeric predictors, term = predictor_label. Standard broom columns: outcome, label, term, estimate_type, estimate, std.error, conf.low, conf.high, statistic, p.value. outcome column carries original variable name; label carries human-readable label. glance() returns one row per outcome model-level statistics. Columns: outcome, label, predictor_type ("categorical" "continuous"), test_type ("F" categorical predictors, "t" continuous ones), statistic, df, df.residual, p.value, r.squared, adj.r.squared, es_type, es_value, es_ci_lower, es_ci_upper, nobs, weighted_n.

Columns: outcome, label, test_type, statistic, df, df.residual, p.value, es_type, es_value, es_ci_lower, es_ci_upper, n_total. Without , outcome, label, n_total populated; columns NA.

title: Tidy / glance methods for spicy_regression_table — tidy.spicy_regression_table
text: tidy() returns one row per (model_id, term, estimate_type) triplet, estimate_type c("B", "beta", "ame", "partial_f2", "partial_eta2", "partial_omega2"). Reference-row placeholders (factor reference levels) singular coefficients (NA estimates) dropped. Columns: model_id, outcome, term, estimate_type, estimate, std.error, conf.low, conf.high, statistic, df, p.value, test_type, is_intercept, factor_term, factor_level. glance() returns one row per (model_id, outcome) model-level statistics. Columns: model_id, outcome, nobs, weighted_nobs, r.squared, adj.r.squared, omega2, sigma, rmse, f2, AIC, AICc, BIC, deviance, df.residual (numeric – Satterthwaite-safe).

title: Uncertainty Coefficient — uncertainty_coef
text: uncertainty_coef() computes Uncertainty Coefficient (Theil's U) two-way contingency table, based information entropy.

title: Uncertainty Coefficient — uncertainty_coef
code: uncertainty_coef(   x,   direction = c("symmetric", "row", "column"),   detail = FALSE,   conf_level = 0.95,   digits = 3L,   .include_se = FALSE )

title: Uncertainty Coefficient — uncertainty_coef
text: x contingency table (class table). direction Direction prediction: "symmetric" (default), "row" (column predicts row), "column" (row predicts column). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Uncertainty Coefficient — uncertainty_coef
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: U = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Uncertainty Coefficient — uncertainty_coef
text: uncertainty coefficient measures association using Shannon entropy. Let \(H_X\) \(H_Y\) marginal entropies row column variables respectively, \(H_{XY}\) joint entropy. direction = "row" (column predicts row): \(U = (H_X + H_Y - H_{XY}) / H_X\). direction = "column" (row predicts column): \(U = (H_X + H_Y - H_{XY}) / H_Y\). direction = "symmetric": \(U = 2 (H_X + H_Y - H_{XY}) / (H_X + H_Y)\). entropy terms use standard mathematical convention \(0 \log 0 = 0\), matching SPSS / PSPP CROSSTABS definition Cover & Thomas (2006). Note DescTools::UncertCoef() applies additional Laplace correction (replacing zero cells \(1/n^2\)) entropy computation, produces slightly different point estimates tables empty cells; correction uncommon information-theory literature used . asymptotic standard errors follow DescTools delta method; see cramer_v() full references.

title: Uncertainty Coefficient — uncertainty_coef
code: tab <- table(sochealth$smoking, sochealth$education) uncertainty_coef(tab) #> [1] 0.01148762 uncertainty_coef(tab, direction = "row", detail = TRUE) #> Estimate  CI lower  CI upper     p #>    0.018     0.003     0.032  .021

what: Generate a comprehensive summary of the variables — varlist
title: Generate a comprehensive summary of the variables — varlist
text: varlist() lists variables data frame extracts essential metadata: variable names, labels, summary values, classes, number distinct values, number valid (non-missing) observations, number missing values. Tidyselect-style selectors can supplied pick reorder columns dynamically. vl() convenient shorthand varlist() offers identical functionality shorter name.

title: Generate a comprehensive summary of the variables — varlist
code: varlist(   x,   ...,   values = FALSE,   tbl = FALSE,   include_na = FALSE,   factor_levels = c("observed", "all") )  vl(   x,   ...,   values = FALSE,   tbl = FALSE,   include_na = FALSE,   factor_levels = c("observed", "all") )

title: Generate a comprehensive summary of the variables — varlist
text: x data frame, transformation one. ... Optional tidyselect-style column selectors (e.g. starts_with("var"), (.numeric), etc.). Columns can selected reordered, renaming selections supported. values Logical. FALSE (default), displays compact summary variable's values. numeric, character, date/time, labelled, factor variables, unique non-missing values shown four; otherwise first three values, ellipsis (...), last value shown. Values sorted appropriate (e.g., numeric, character, date). factors, factor_levels controls whether observed declared levels shown; level order preserved. labelled variables, prefixed labels displayed via labelled::to_factor(levels = "prefixed"). TRUE, unique non-missing values displayed. tbl Logical. FALSE (default), opens summary Viewer session interactive. TRUE, returns tibble. include_na Logical. TRUE, unique missing value markers (<NA>, <NaN>) explicitly appended end Values summary present variable. applies variable types. Literal strings "NA", "NaN", "" quoted distinguish missing markers. FALSE (default), missing values omitted Values still counted NAs column. factor_levels Character. Controls factor values displayed Values. "observed" (default; code_book() uses "") shows levels present data, preserving factor level order. "" shows declared levels, including unused levels.

title: Generate a comprehensive summary of the variables — varlist
text: tibble one row per selected variable, containing following columns: Variable: variable names Label: variable labels (available via label attribute) Values: summary variable's values, depending values include_na arguments. values = FALSE, compact summary shown: unique values four, otherwise 3 + ... + last. values = TRUE, unique non-missing values displayed. labelled variables, prefixed labels displayed using labelled::to_factor(levels = "prefixed"). factors, levels displayed according factor_levels. Matrix array columns summarized dimensions. Missing value markers (<NA>, <NaN>) optionally appended end (controlled via include_na). Literal strings "NA", "NaN", "" quoted distinguish missing markers. Class: class variable (possibly multiple, e.g. "labelled", "numeric") N_distinct: number distinct non-missing values N_valid: number non-missing observations NAs: number missing observations matrix array columns, observations counted per row: row treated missing cells NA. N_valid / NAs therefore count complete vs. incomplete rows, individual cells. tbl = FALSE (default) tibble sent Viewer (interactive) surfaced via message (non-interactive) function returns invisibly NULL. Set tbl = TRUE return tibble directly downstream use.

title: Generate a comprehensive summary of the variables — varlist
text: interactive session (RStudio, Positron, ...), summary opens Viewer pane contextual title like vl: sochealth. data frame transformed subsetted, title suffixed * (e.g. vl: sochealth*); anonymous ambiguous calls fall back vl: <data>. Pass tbl = TRUE return tibble instead. default factor_levels = "observed" mirrors actually data; code_book() defaults "" document declared schema. See @param factor_levels override either default.

title: Yule's Q — yule_q
text: yule_q() computes Yule's Q coefficient association 2x2 contingency table.

title: Yule's Q — yule_q
code: yule_q(x, detail = FALSE, conf_level = 0.95, digits = 3L, .include_se = FALSE)

title: Yule's Q — yule_q
text: x contingency table (class table). detail Logical. FALSE (default), return estimate numeric scalar. TRUE, return named numeric vector including confidence interval p-value. conf_level number 0 1 giving confidence level (default 0.95). used detail = TRUE. Set NULL omit confidence interval. digits Number decimal places used printing result (default 3). affects detail = TRUE output. .include_se Internal parameter; use.

title: Yule's Q — yule_q
text: structure cramer_v(): scalar detail = FALSE, named vector detail = TRUE. p-value tests H0: Q = 0 (Wald z-test).

title: Yule's Q — yule_q
text: 2x2 table cells \(, b, c, d\), Yule's Q \(Q = (ad - bc) / (ad + bc)\). equivalent Goodman-Kruskal Gamma 2x2 tables. asymptotic standard error \(SE = 0.5 (1 - Q^2) \sqrt{1/+ 1/b + 1/c + 1/d}\). Edge cases: ad + bc = 0, Q undefined function returns NA spicy_undefined_stat warning. cell zero (ad + bc > 0), Q well-defined SE formula divides zero – point estimate returned, se, ci_lower, ci_upper, p_value NA. Standard error formulas follow DescTools implementations (Signorell et al., 2024); see cramer_v() full references.

title: Yule's Q — yule_q

title: spicy 0.12.0

title: spicy 0.12.0
text: New table_regression(): publication-ready coefficient summary one fitted lm glm models, side side. APA Manual 7 formatting default. Highlights: Robust variance: classical, HC, cluster-robust (CR) Satterthwaite df, bootstrap, jackknife. Per-model vcov accepted SE-comparison tables. Standardisation: refit, posthoc, basic, smart, pseudo (last glm ). Average marginal effects (AME) separate columns; AME inference shares coefficient’s variance estimator B AME reported inferential footing. Partial effect sizes: f², η², ω² lm (noncentral-F CIs); partial χ² glm. GLM response-scale reporting via exponentiate = TRUE, family-appropriate labels (, IRR, HR, RR, MR, exp(B)) optional profile-likelihood CIs (ci_method = "profile"). Multiplicity correction via p_adjust (stats::p.adjust() method). Hierarchical comparison via nested = TRUE (ΔR² / F-change lm; LRT glm). Display controls: variable filtering, intercept factor placement, reference-row styles, multi-model labels, stars, decimal mark, per-column digits. Outputs: console, data.frame, long tibble, gt, flextable, tinytable, Excel, Word, clipboard. broom::tidy() broom::glance() methods supported. See ?table_regression vignette("table-regression"). table_continuous_lm() gains additive covariate adjustment via new covariates argument. Two estimands per-group adjusted means: "proportional" (G-computation, default) "balanced" (equal-weight synthetic grid). adjustment, f² ω² become partial effect sizes; d g raise explanatory error. auto-built footer documents covariates estimand. See vignette("table-continuous-lm"). New exported as_structured() accessor returns typed view table_regression() result programmatic use: raw numerics, CI split LL / UL columns, column-level format specification.

text: copy_clipboard() parameter message renamed show_message. freq() now dispatches printing correctly via S3. Removed unused collapse stringi Imports.

title: spicy 0.4.2
text: CRAN release: 2026-03-06 cross_tab() hardening: improved vector-mode detection (including labelled vectors), stricter weight validation, safer rescaling, clearer early errors (e.g., explicit y = NULL). cross_tab() statistics now computed non-empty margins grouped tables, avoiding spurious NA results; internal core path refactored remove dplyr/tibble computation preserving user-facing behavior. freq() now errors clearly x missing data.frame input validates rescaling weight sums zero/non-finite. count_n(), mean_n(), sum_n() regex mode hardened (regex = TRUE now validates/defaults select safely). mean_n() sum_n() now return NA (warning) numeric columns selected. label_from_names() now validates input type (data.frame/tibble required). cramer_v() now returns NA warning degenerate tables. Dependency optimization: DT clipr moved Suggests; optional runtime checks added code_book() copy_clipboard(). Tests expanded regression coverage edge cases.

title: spicy 0.4.1
text: CRAN release: 2025-12-21 Fixed CRAN incoming check notes removing non-standard top-level files.

title: spicy 0.4.0
text: Print methods fully redesigned produce clean, aligned ASCII tables inspired Stata’s layout. new implementation improves formatting, adds optional color support, provides consistent handling totals column spacing. Output freq() cross_tab() now benefits enhanced print.spicy() formatting, offering clearer, readable summary tables. Documentation internal tests updated clarity consistency. cross_tab() gains explicit correct argument control use Yates’ continuity correction Chi-squared tests 2x2 tables. default behavior remains unchanged. documentation cross_tab() refined harmonized, clearer high-level description, improved parameter wording, expanded examples. Minor cosmetic improvements made varlist() output: title prefix now uses vl: instead VARLIST, column name Ndist_val renamed N_distinct improved readability consistency. Minor cosmetic improvement: ASCII table output longer includes closing bottom rule default.

title: spicy 0.3.0
text: CRAN release: 2025-10-22 New function code_book(), generates comprehensive variable codebook can viewed interactively exported multiple formats (copy, print, CSV, Excel, PDF).

title: spicy 0.2.1
text: CRAN release: 2025-10-04 label_from_names() now correctly handles edge cases separator appears label missing.

title: spicy 0.2.0
text: CRAN release: 2025-09-25 New function label_from_names() derive assign variable labels headers form "name<sep>label" (e.g. "name. label"). Especially useful LimeSurvey CSV exports (Export results -> CSV -> Headings: Question code & question text), default separator ". ".

title: spicy 0.1.0
text: CRAN release: 2025-05-05

title: spicy 0.1.0
text: Introduces collection tools variable inspection, descriptive summaries, data exploration. Extract variable metadata display compact summaries (varlist()). Compute frequency tables (freq()), cross-tabulations (cross_tab()), Cramer’s V categorical associations (cramer_v()). Generate descriptive statistics means (mean_n()), sums (sum_n()), counts (count_n()) automatic handling missing data. Copy data (copy_clipboard()) directly clipboard quick export.
